foundingEvent.title: The Founding Charter
foundingEvent.theme: linear vs circular metabolism; the waste hierarchy
foundingEvent.brief: Your city is young, but it's growing fast — new families arrive every week and the bins are filling quicker than anyone expected. The council gathers for its very first big decision: *how should this city deal with its rubbish, as a rule?* Everyone is watching. Whatever you decide today will shape the roads, factories, habits and budgets of the city for years to come. There's no going back to a blank page after this.
foundingEvent.caseFact: A city's "metabolism" can be linear (resources flow in, waste flows out and is discarded) or circular (waste is fed back as a resource). The waste hierarchy ranks strategies from most preferred (reduce) to least (landfill).

entry.theme: linear vs circular metabolism; the waste hierarchy

actions.label: Just bury it — keep it cheap
actions.resultExplain: Cheapest option, so you keep money for other things The land fills up and runs out
actions.plainLabel: Just bury it — keep it cheap
actions.plainMeaning: Collect the rubbish and bury it in a tip. The simplest, cheapest way to start.
pros: Cheapest option, so you keep money for other things
quick and easy to set up
makes space problems disappear for now.
cons: The land fills up and runs out
buried rubbish pollutes soil, air and water over time
you're building lazy "throw-it-away" habits that are hard to undo later.
actions.conceptLink: linear vs circular city — do you treat waste as rubbish to get rid of, or as a resource to reuse?

actions.label: Burn it for electricity
actions.resultExplain: Shrinks the rubbish pile dramatically Expensive to build
actions.plainMeaning: Build a high-tech furnace that burns rubbish and turns the heat into power.
pros: makes electricity you can use or sell
needs far less land than a tip.
cons: the chimney emits pollution that worries nearby residents
you still have to deal with the toxic ash left behind.
actions.conceptLink: linear vs circular city — do you treat waste as rubbish to get rid of, or as a resource to reuse?

actions.label: Sort and recycle it
actions.resultExplain: Turns waste into useful materials and income Costs more to set up
actions.plainMeaning: Ask people to separate their rubbish so materials can be recovered and sold or reused.
pros: Turns waste into useful materials and income
builds a city that bounces back from shocks.
cons: only works if residents actually sort properly
the payoff builds slowly, not overnight.
actions.conceptLink: linear vs circular city — do you treat waste as rubbish to get rid of, or as a resource to reuse?

actions.label: Stop the waste before it starts
actions.resultExplain: The most powerful fix — rubbish you prevent costs nothing to handle The most expensive and slowest to show results
actions.plainLabel: Stop the waste before it starts
actions.plainMeaning: Push to reduce packaging and throwaway goods so less rubbish is ever created.
pros: The most powerful fix — rubbish you prevent costs nothing to handle
biggest long-term win for the environment
positions you as a zero-waste leader.
cons: The most expensive and slowest to show results
businesses and shoppers resist new rules
actions.conceptLink: linear vs circular city — do you treat waste as rubbish to get rid of, or as a resource to reuse?

justify.question: A council adopts a 'collect-and-bury' approach as its founding waste strategy. Which statement BEST explains why this is the least aligned with sustainable urban development?
options: It treats discarded material as a resource, closing the materials loop
It sustains a linear throughput of resources and externalises long-term costs onto future land, air and water
It is the most expensive option and immediately strains the municipal budget
It maximises citizen participation in waste reduction
optionExplanations: Wrong: closing the loop describes a circular metabolism; burying waste discards materials and is linear.
Correct: linear disposal keeps resource throughput high and shifts costs (leachate, methane, lost land) to the future and surrounding areas.
Wrong: disposal is the cheapest option up front; its costs are long-term and externalised, not an immediate budget hit.
Wrong: burial requires no behaviour change — participation is associated with reduce/recycle strategies, not disposal.

foundingEvent.scene: Your city is young, but it's growing fast — new families arrive every week and the bins are filling quicker than anyone expected. The council gathers for its very first big decision: *how should this city deal with its rubbish, as a rule?* Everyone is watching. Whatever you decide today will shape the roads, factories, habits and budgets of the city for years to come. There's no going back to a blank page after this.
foundingEvent.conceptLink: linear vs circular city — do you treat waste as rubbish to get rid of, or as a resource to reuse?

easy.question: Which approach treats rubbish as a resource instead of just throwing it away?
options: Recycling and reducing waste
Burying everything in a tip
Throwing it in a river
Ignoring it
optionExplanations: Right: recycling and reducing keep materials in use — a circular city.
Wrong: burying discards materials — that's linear.
Wrong: river dumping pollutes and wastes materials.
Wrong: ignoring waste solves nothing.

standard.question: A council adopts a 'collect-and-bury' approach as its founding waste strategy. Which statement BEST explains why this is the least aligned with sustainable urban development?
options: It treats discarded material as a resource, closing the materials loop
It sustains a linear throughput of resources and externalises long-term costs onto future land, air and water
It is the most expensive option and immediately strains the municipal budget
It maximises citizen participation in waste reduction
optionExplanations: Wrong: closing the loop describes a circular metabolism; burying waste discards materials and is linear.
Correct: linear disposal keeps resource throughput high and shifts costs (leachate, methane, lost land) to the future and surrounding areas.
Wrong: disposal is the cheapest option up front; its costs are long-term and externalised, not an immediate budget hit.
Wrong: burial requires no behaviour change — participation is associated with reduce/recycle strategies, not disposal.

hard.question: A planner argues disposal-led management is 'most rational' for a fast-growing low-income city with cheap land. What is the strongest sustainability counter-argument?
options: Even with cheap land, disposal externalises rising leachate, methane and health costs that compound as the city grows
Disposal is more expensive than recycling from the very first day
Cheap land is never available in low-income cities
Disposal prevents any economic growth at all
optionExplanations: Correct: the costs are deferred and externalised, growing with the city — the core sustainability objection.
Wrong: disposal is cheapest up front; the issue is long-term cost.
Wrong: an overgeneralisation; such land often does exist.
Wrong: overstated — disposal does not preclude growth.

roundEvents.title: The Convenience Trap
roundEvents.brief: Walk down any high street and everything is wrapped in plastic — fruit in trays, drinks in double layers, snacks in pouches inside boxes. Shops love it because shiny packaging sells, but your bins are overflowing overnight. Residents are starting to notice the mountains of wrapping on collection day.
roundEvents.caseFact: Refrigeration and global transport made convenience packaging routine — packaging is now a marketing tool, not just protection.

actions.label: Charge a small fee on excess packaging
actions.resultExplain: Cuts rubbish at the source Shops and shoppers grumble about higher prices
actions.plainLabel: Charge a small fee on excess packaging
actions.plainMeaning: Make shops pay a little for wasteful wrapping, nudging them to use less.
cons: Shops and shoppers grumble about higher prices
the benefit takes time to show.
actions.conceptLink: where waste comes from — affluence and convenience packaging.

actions.label: Run a 'bring your own bag/cup' campaign
actions.resultExplain: Cheap and popular Only some people join in
actions.plainLabel: Run a 'bring your own bag/cup' campaign
actions.plainMeaning: Encourage people to refuse single-use packaging voluntarily.
cons: the effect is small and slow.
actions.conceptLink: where waste comes from — affluence and convenience packaging.

actions.label: Do nothing, just collect more
actions.resultExplain: No backlash Rubbish keeps climbing
actions.plainMeaning: Leave shops alone and keep picking up the extra rubbish.
pros: keeps shops and shoppers happy today.
cons: you've locked in a throwaway culture.
actions.conceptLink: where waste comes from — affluence and convenience packaging.

justify.question: Reduction sits above recycling in the waste hierarchy. Which reasoning BEST justifies that ranking?
options: Recycling is technically impossible for most packaging
Preventing waste avoids the energy, emissions and cost of later collecting, sorting and reprocessing it
Reduction generates more recyclable material to sell
Recycling inevitably contaminates groundwater
optionExplanations: Wrong: most packaging is recyclable; technical impossibility is not the basis of the hierarchy.
Correct: waste never created needs no downstream handling, so prevention avoids all those costs and impacts.
Wrong: reduction lowers the amount of material entering the stream — the opposite of generating more.
Wrong: leachate is a landfill issue, not an inherent feature of recycling.

roundEvents.scene: Walk down any high street and everything is wrapped in plastic — fruit in trays, drinks in double layers, snacks in pouches inside boxes. Shops love it because shiny packaging sells, but your bins are overflowing overnight. Residents are starting to notice the mountains of wrapping on collection day.
roundEvents.conceptLink: where waste comes from — affluence and convenience packaging.

easy.question: Why is using less packaging better than recycling it?
options: Less waste is created in the first place
It makes more rubbish
It is completely free
Recycling is banned
optionExplanations: Right: prevented waste never needs handling.
Wrong: reducing means less waste, not more.
Wrong: it has some cost, but avoids waste.
Wrong: recycling isn't banned.

standard.question: Reduction sits above recycling in the waste hierarchy. Which reasoning BEST justifies that ranking?
options: Recycling is technically impossible for most packaging
Preventing waste avoids the energy, emissions and cost of later collecting, sorting and reprocessing it
Reduction generates more recyclable material to sell
Recycling inevitably contaminates groundwater
optionExplanations: Wrong: most packaging is recyclable; technical impossibility is not the basis of the hierarchy.
Correct: waste never created needs no downstream handling, so prevention avoids all those costs and impacts.
Wrong: reduction lowers the amount of material entering the stream — the opposite of generating more.
Wrong: leachate is a landfill issue, not an inherent feature of recycling.

hard.question: Critics call a packaging tax 'slow and ineffective'. Which response best defends it on sustainability grounds?
options: By internalising disposal costs it shifts producer and consumer behaviour toward less packaging, even if benefits lag
It immediately eliminates all packaging waste
It guarantees higher recycling revenue this year
It has no effect on behaviour
optionExplanations: Correct: pricing externalities changes behaviour over time — the lag is expected, not a refutation.
Wrong: overstated; change is gradual.
Wrong: it targets reduction, not short-term revenue.
Wrong: that contradicts the rationale for the tax.

roundEvents.title: Built to be Binned
roundEvents.brief: Cheap gadgets and clothes are everywhere, and they're designed to be tossed, not fixed — when a phone battery dies, it's cheaper to buy a new phone than repair it. Landfills are filling with stuff that barely got used. People shrug; that's just how things are now.
roundEvents.caseFact: Many industries rely on built-in obsolescence — replacement is cheaper than repair, driving constant waste.

actions.label: Fund repair cafés and reuse shops
actions.resultExplain: Extends the life of products Costs money up front
actions.plainLabel: Fund repair cafés and reuse shops
actions.plainMeaning: Set up places where people can fix and re-sell things instead of binning them.
cons: only works if people choose to use them.
actions.conceptLink: "built-in obsolescence" — products made to be replaced, driving endless waste.

actions.label: Pass a 'right to repair' rule
actions.resultExplain: Tackles the root cause Manufacturers lobby hard against it
actions.plainMeaning: Force makers to design products that can actually be fixed.
actions.conceptLink: "built-in obsolescence" — products made to be replaced, driving endless waste.

actions.label: Just collect the extra rubbish
actions.resultExplain: Keeps streets tidy now Treats the symptom, not the cause
actions.plainMeaning: Expand collection to cope with all the discarded goods.
cons: Treats the symptom, not the cause
actions.conceptLink: "built-in obsolescence" — products made to be replaced, driving endless waste.

justify.question: Why does a 'right to repair' law tackle the waste problem more fundamentally than simply expanding collection?
options: It extends product lifespans, reducing the volume of goods entering the waste stream
It increases manufacturing output to meet demand
It removes the need for any recycling system
It lowers collection costs without changing consumption
optionExplanations: Correct: durable, repairable goods are replaced less often, cutting waste at its structural source.
Wrong: it slows replacement demand, so it tends to reduce, not increase, output.
Wrong: it reduces but does not eliminate end-of-life waste; recycling is still needed.
Wrong: that describes a collection efficiency, not addressing the root driver (built-in obsolescence).

roundEvents.scene: Cheap gadgets and clothes are everywhere, and they're designed to be tossed, not fixed — when a phone battery dies, it's cheaper to buy a new phone than repair it. Landfills are filling with stuff that barely got used. People shrug; that's just how things are now.
roundEvents.conceptLink: "built-in obsolescence" — products made to be replaced, driving endless waste.

easy.question: What do cheap goods 'made to be thrown away' do to a city's rubbish?
options: Increase it
Decrease it
Have no effect
Recycle it automatically
optionExplanations: Right: constant replacement piles up waste.
Wrong: they add waste, not reduce it.
Wrong: they clearly add to the bins.
Wrong: throwaway goods aren't auto-recycled.

standard.question: Why does a 'right to repair' law tackle the waste problem more fundamentally than simply expanding collection?
options: It extends product lifespans, reducing the volume of goods entering the waste stream
It increases manufacturing output to meet demand
It removes the need for any recycling system
It lowers collection costs without changing consumption
optionExplanations: Correct: durable, repairable goods are replaced less often, cutting waste at its structural source.
Wrong: it slows replacement demand, so it tends to reduce, not increase, output.
Wrong: it reduces but does not eliminate end-of-life waste; recycling is still needed.
Wrong: that describes a collection efficiency, not addressing the root driver (built-in obsolescence).

hard.question: Why might 'right to repair' regulation cut waste more durably than subsidising repair cafés?
options: It changes product design upstream, addressing the structural cause rather than only the symptom
Repair cafés increase manufacturing output
Regulation removes the need for any recycling
Subsidies always outperform regulation
optionExplanations: Correct: mandating repairable design tackles obsolescence at source, a more durable fix.
Wrong: cafés extend product life, not raise output.
Wrong: recycling is still needed.
Wrong: unsupported absolute claim.

roundEvents.title: Lockdown Leftovers
roundEvents.brief: Food delivery has exploded — every meal arrives in a stack of plastic tubs, bags and cutlery. In just two months the city has buried the equivalent of dozens of bus-loads of extra plastic. It's convenient, but the bins tell the story.
roundEvents.caseFact: Singapore households generated +1,334 tonnes of plastic during a two-month lockdown from deliveries.

actions.label: Launch a reusable container scheme
actions.resultExplain: Cuts packaging waste for good Needs a return-and-wash system to work
actions.plainMeaning: Set up returnable tubs so meals don't need throwaway packaging.
cons: Needs a return-and-wash system to work
actions.conceptLink: consumption habits spiking waste (the COVID delivery surge).

actions.label: Add recycling bins at food courts
actions.resultExplain: Captures materials if people sort right Lots of food-soiled plastic can't be recycled
actions.plainLabel: Add recycling bins at food courts
actions.plainMeaning: Put clearly-labelled bins where the takeaway waste is created.
pros: Captures materials if people sort right
cons: Lots of food-soiled plastic can't be recycled
actions.conceptLink: consumption habits spiking waste (the COVID delivery surge).

actions.label: Send it all to the tip
actions.resultExplain: No effort or cost now. Plastic lasts centuries
actions.plainLabel: Send it all to the tip
actions.plainMeaning: Just bury the extra packaging.
actions.conceptLink: consumption habits spiking waste (the COVID delivery surge).

justify.question: The delivery surge raised plastic waste sharply. Why is this difficult to solve through recycling alone?
options: Food-contaminated single-use plastics are often non-recyclable, keeping capture rates low
Most delivery packaging is biodegradable
Recycling mixed plastics is always profitable
Delivery packaging is predominantly glass
optionExplanations: Correct: contamination by food makes much of this plastic uneconomic or impossible to recycle, so it leaks to disposal.
Wrong: the packaging is largely non-biodegradable plastic.
Wrong: mixed-plastic recycling is frequently loss-making and price-volatile.
Wrong: it is overwhelmingly plastic, not glass.

roundEvents.scene: Food delivery has exploded — every meal arrives in a stack of plastic tubs, bags and cutlery. In just two months the city has buried the equivalent of dozens of bus-loads of extra plastic. It's convenient, but the bins tell the story.
roundEvents.conceptLink: consumption habits spiking waste (the COVID delivery surge).

easy.question: Why is takeaway packaging often hard to recycle?
options: It is dirty with food
It is made of gold
It is always spotless
It disappears by itself
optionExplanations: Right: food contamination makes much of it non-recyclable.
Wrong: it's cheap plastic, not gold.
Wrong: it's usually soiled.
Wrong: plastic does not vanish.

standard.question: The delivery surge raised plastic waste sharply. Why is this difficult to solve through recycling alone?
options: Food-contaminated single-use plastics are often non-recyclable, keeping capture rates low
Most delivery packaging is biodegradable
Recycling mixed plastics is always profitable
Delivery packaging is predominantly glass
optionExplanations: Correct: contamination by food makes much of this plastic uneconomic or impossible to recycle, so it leaks to disposal.
Wrong: the packaging is largely non-biodegradable plastic.
Wrong: mixed-plastic recycling is frequently loss-making and price-volatile.
Wrong: it is overwhelmingly plastic, not glass.

hard.question: A city adds recycling points for takeaway waste but capture stays low. What is the most likely explanation?
options: Food contamination makes much single-use plastic unrecyclable regardless of bin provision
Residents recycle too enthusiastically
Plastic is biodegradable, so it isn't collected
Recycling points reduce the waste generated
optionExplanations: Correct: contamination, not bin availability, is the binding constraint here.
Wrong: nonsensical as a cause of low capture.
Wrong: the plastic is non-biodegradable.
Wrong: conflates collection points with source reduction.

roundEvents.title: Edge City
roundEvents.brief: A brand-new neighbourhood is rising at the city's edge — homes are filling up before the bin lorries even have a route out there. Residents are already leaving bags on the kerb with nowhere for them to go. You can shape how this suburb deals with waste from day one.
roundEvents.caseFact: Urban sprawl pushes infrastructure outward; fringe areas often lack basic services first.

actions.label: Build full sorting + collection from day one
actions.resultExplain: Designs good habits in from the start Most expensive option up front.
actions.plainLabel: Build full sorting + collection from day one
actions.plainMeaning: Give the new suburb proper recycling and collection immediately.
pros: Designs good habits in from the start
actions.conceptLink: urban sprawl and getting services to the fringe.

actions.label: Basic bin collection only
actions.resultExplain: Cheaper Locks in throwaway habits
actions.plainMeaning: Just send lorries to pick up mixed rubbish.
actions.conceptLink: urban sprawl and getting services to the fringe.

actions.label: Let residents sort it out themselves
actions.resultExplain: Saves money today. Illegal dumping and resentment grow
actions.plainLabel: Let residents sort it out themselves
actions.plainMeaning: Leave the new suburb to manage its own waste for now.
actions.conceptLink: urban sprawl and getting services to the fringe.

justify.question: Why is building waste infrastructure into a new district from the outset more sustainable than retrofitting later?
options: It locks in collection-and-disposal patterns and infrastructure that are costly to reverse once established
New districts generate no waste in their first years
Retrofitting is always cheaper than building new
It removes the need for any collection service
optionExplanations: Correct: path dependency means early infrastructure and habits shape behaviour for decades; changing them later is expensive.
Wrong: occupied homes generate waste immediately.
Wrong: retrofitting established systems is generally more expensive and disruptive.
Wrong: collection is still required regardless of design.

roundEvents.scene: A brand-new neighbourhood is rising at the city's edge — homes are filling up before the bin lorries even have a route out there. Residents are already leaving bags on the kerb with nowhere for them to go. You can shape how this suburb deals with waste from day one.
roundEvents.conceptLink: urban sprawl and getting services to the fringe.

easy.question: Why set up recycling in a new neighbourhood from the start?
options: Habits are hard to change later
New areas make no waste
Fixing later is always cheaper
It removes the need for bins
optionExplanations: Right: early habits and infrastructure stick.
Wrong: occupied homes make waste at once.
Wrong: retrofitting is usually pricier.
Wrong: collection is still needed.

standard.question: Why is building waste infrastructure into a new district from the outset more sustainable than retrofitting later?
options: It locks in collection-and-disposal patterns and infrastructure that are costly to reverse once established
New districts generate no waste in their first years
Retrofitting is always cheaper than building new
It removes the need for any collection service
optionExplanations: Correct: path dependency means early infrastructure and habits shape behaviour for decades; changing them later is expensive.
Wrong: occupied homes generate waste immediately.
Wrong: retrofitting established systems is generally more expensive and disruptive.
Wrong: collection is still required regardless of design.

hard.question: Designing circular systems into a new district is defended on 'path dependency'. What does that mean?
options: Early infrastructure and habits become self-reinforcing, making later change costly
New districts are legally exempt from waste rules
Waste systems have no influence on behaviour
Costs always fall over time regardless of design
optionExplanations: Correct: initial choices lock in trajectories that are expensive to reverse.
Wrong: no such exemption exists.
Wrong: infrastructure shapes behaviour.
Wrong: retrofitting is typically costlier.

roundEvents.title: Streets of Refuse
roundEvents.brief: In the older, crowded districts the bin lorries simply can't keep up. Rubbish piles on corners, sometimes catching fire in the heat, and rats and flies are moving in. Families living there are getting sick, and they're angry.
roundEvents.caseFact: The 2012 World Bank report found 30–60% of urban waste in lower-income countries is uncollected.

actions.label: Invest in more lorries and routes
actions.resultExplain: Quickly removes the health hazard Costs money
actions.plainLabel: Invest in more lorries and routes
actions.plainMeaning: Expand the collection fleet to clear the backlog.
cons: you still have to dispose of all that waste somewhere.
actions.conceptLink: uncollected waste in fast-growing cities and its health toll.

actions.label: Hire local informal collectors
actions.resultExplain: Cheap and fast Needs organising and support to be safe and fair.
actions.plainMeaning: Pay the people already collecting waste by cart to do it officially.
pros: boosts recycling and gives people fair work
cons: Needs organising and support to be safe and fair.
actions.conceptLink: uncollected waste in fast-growing cities and its health toll.

actions.label: Leave it for now
actions.resultExplain: Saves money this year. Disease spreads
actions.plainMeaning: Don't act; focus the budget elsewhere.
actions.conceptLink: uncollected waste in fast-growing cities and its health toll.

justify.question: Uncollected waste in dense low-income districts is primarily a sustainability concern because it...
options: creates disease vectors and contaminates water, undermining the social and environmental pillars of SUD
directly lowers national GDP figures
is purely an aesthetic nuisance
raises recycling rates through informal picking
optionExplanations: Correct: accumulating waste breeds pests and pollutes water, harming health (social) and ecosystems (environmental).
Wrong: any GDP effect is indirect; the core concern is health and environmental harm.
Wrong: it is a serious health hazard, not merely unsightly.
Wrong: informal picking may occur, but it does not offset the public-health emergency and is not the primary concern.

roundEvents.scene: In the older, crowded districts the bin lorries simply can't keep up. Rubbish piles on corners, sometimes catching fire in the heat, and rats and flies are moving in. Families living there are getting sick, and they're angry.
roundEvents.conceptLink: uncollected waste in fast-growing cities and its health toll.

easy.question: Why is rubbish left uncollected in the streets dangerous?
options: It spreads disease and pests
It only looks untidy
It keeps people healthy
It has no effect
optionExplanations: Right: piles breed pests and pollute water.
Wrong: it's a health hazard, not just messy.
Wrong: it harms, not helps, health.
Wrong: it has serious effects.

standard.question: Uncollected waste in dense low-income districts is primarily a sustainability concern because it...
options: creates disease vectors and contaminates water, undermining the social and environmental pillars of SUD
directly lowers national GDP figures
is purely an aesthetic nuisance
raises recycling rates through informal picking
optionExplanations: Correct: accumulating waste breeds pests and pollutes water, harming health (social) and ecosystems (environmental).
Wrong: any GDP effect is indirect; the core concern is health and environmental harm.
Wrong: it is a serious health hazard, not merely unsightly.
Wrong: informal picking may occur, but it does not offset the public-health emergency and is not the primary concern.

hard.question: In low-income cities, which mechanism most directly turns uncollected waste into a public-health crisis?
options: Disease vectors breeding in waste while leachate contaminates water supplies
The aesthetic decline of neighbourhoods
Reduced property-tax revenue
Increased informal recycling activity
optionExplanations: Correct: vectors and water contamination are the direct disease pathways.
Wrong: appearance is secondary to health.
Wrong: a fiscal effect, not the health mechanism.
Wrong: a side-effect, not the cause of the crisis.

roundEvents.title: Sort It Out
roundEvents.brief: Your team proposes a city-wide scheme: give every household separate bins for paper, glass, plastic and food scraps, and make sorting the easy, normal thing to do. It could transform how the city handles waste — if people get on board.
roundEvents.caseFact: Copenhagen made source separation an easy default at household level for paper, glass, metal, plastic, organics.

actions.label: Full kerbside sorting for every home
actions.resultExplain: Big jump in recycling Expensive to launch
actions.plainLabel: Full kerbside sorting for every home
actions.plainMeaning: Roll out separate bins citywide and collect each stream.
cons: flops if households don't sort properly.
actions.conceptLink: household recycling only works with real participation.

actions.label: Just set up drop-off points
actions.resultExplain: Much cheaper Lower take-up
actions.plainMeaning: Place recycling banks around the city for people to use.
actions.conceptLink: household recycling only works with real participation.

actions.label: Wait until the budget is healthier
actions.resultExplain: Saves money today. You lose early momentum and stay a throwaway city.
actions.plainLabel: Wait until the budget is healthier
actions.plainMeaning: Delay recycling for now.
cons: You lose early momentum and stay a throwaway city.
actions.conceptLink: household recycling only works with real participation.

justify.question: Heavily-funded source-separation schemes can still fail. Which factor is the decisive variable?
options: Citizen participation and low contamination, since mixed/contaminated streams cannot be economically reprocessed
The colour scheme of the bins
The size of the municipal budget on its own
Proximity of homes to the nearest landfill
optionExplanations: Correct: recycling economics depend on clean, well-sorted material; without participation, contamination collapses the system.
Wrong: bin design is marginal compared with sorting behaviour.
Wrong: money buys infrastructure but cannot force correct sorting — participation is the binding constraint.
Wrong: distance to landfill does not determine recycling success.

roundEvents.scene: Your team proposes a city-wide scheme: give every household separate bins for paper, glass, plastic and food scraps, and make sorting the easy, normal thing to do. It could transform how the city handles waste — if people get on board.
roundEvents.conceptLink: household recycling only works with real participation.

easy.question: Recycling bins only work well if...
options: people actually sort their waste
the bins are a nice colour
the city is rich
a landfill is nearby
optionExplanations: Right: sorting by residents is essential.
Wrong: colour barely matters.
Wrong: money alone can't force sorting.
Wrong: landfill distance is irrelevant.

standard.question: Heavily-funded source-separation schemes can still fail. Which factor is the decisive variable?
options: Citizen participation and low contamination, since mixed/contaminated streams cannot be economically reprocessed
The colour scheme of the bins
The size of the municipal budget on its own
Proximity of homes to the nearest landfill
optionExplanations: Correct: recycling economics depend on clean, well-sorted material; without participation, contamination collapses the system.
Wrong: bin design is marginal compared with sorting behaviour.
Wrong: money buys infrastructure but cannot force correct sorting — participation is the binding constraint.
Wrong: distance to landfill does not determine recycling success.

hard.question: Two cities invest equally in source separation; one recycles far more. The decisive difference is most likely...
options: participation and contamination levels, which determine how much material is truly recoverable
the brand of collection vehicles
proximity to the nearest incinerator
the size of the city's debt
optionExplanations: Correct: behaviour and material quality drive recovery once investment is equal.
Wrong: vehicle brand is immaterial.
Wrong: incinerator distance is irrelevant.
Wrong: debt does not determine recycling capture.

roundEvents.title: Factory Lines
roundEvents.brief: A new industrial park has opened, bringing jobs and tax money — and a flood of commercial and factory waste that's very different from household rubbish. Some of it is bulky, some hazardous, all of it lands on your disposal system.
roundEvents.caseFact: Urban waste is not just domestic; it comes from industrial and commercial activity too.

actions.label: Require factories to audit and reuse waste
actions.resultExplain: Pushes industry to be circular Some firms complain about red tape.
actions.plainLabel: Require factories to audit and reuse waste
actions.plainMeaning: Make businesses track their waste and reuse what they can.
cons: Some firms complain about red tape.
actions.conceptLink: city waste is industrial and commercial, not just domestic.

actions.label: Charge factories a disposal fee
actions.resultExplain: Raises useful revenue Some firms may cut corners or dump illegally.
actions.plainMeaning: Make businesses pay for what they throw away.
pros: gives a mild nudge to waste less.
cons: Some firms may cut corners or dump illegally.
actions.conceptLink: city waste is industrial and commercial, not just domestic.

actions.label: Take their waste into the general system
actions.resultExplain: Keeps businesses happy and attracts more. Overloads your disposal
actions.plainLabel: Take their waste into the general system
actions.plainMeaning: Handle factory waste alongside household rubbish.
pros: Keeps businesses happy and attracts more.
actions.conceptLink: city waste is industrial and commercial, not just domestic.

justify.question: Planning a city's waste system only around household rubbish is inadequate because...
options: industrial and commercial activity generate large, varied and sometimes hazardous streams the system must handle
households are the sole source of hazardous waste
industry produces no waste of its own
commercial waste is automatically recycled
optionExplanations: Correct: urban waste includes substantial industrial/commercial flows with different handling needs.
Wrong: industry, not households, is the main source of many hazardous wastes.
Wrong: industrial processes are significant waste generators.
Wrong: commercial waste is not recycled by default; it must be managed.

roundEvents.scene: A new industrial park has opened, bringing jobs and tax money — and a flood of commercial and factory waste that's very different from household rubbish. Some of it is bulky, some hazardous, all of it lands on your disposal system.
roundEvents.conceptLink: city waste is industrial and commercial, not just domestic.

easy.question: Besides homes, what else creates a city's waste?
options: Factories and businesses
Only households
Nothing else
Rivers
optionExplanations: Right: industry and commerce produce lots of waste.
Wrong: homes aren't the only source.
Wrong: many sources exist.
Wrong: rivers aren't a waste source.

standard.question: Planning a city's waste system only around household rubbish is inadequate because...
options: industrial and commercial activity generate large, varied and sometimes hazardous streams the system must handle
households are the sole source of hazardous waste
industry produces no waste of its own
commercial waste is automatically recycled
optionExplanations: Correct: urban waste includes substantial industrial/commercial flows with different handling needs.
Wrong: industry, not households, is the main source of many hazardous wastes.
Wrong: industrial processes are significant waste generators.
Wrong: commercial waste is not recycled by default; it must be managed.

hard.question: Why can attracting industry boost the economy yet worsen the sustainability scorecard at once?
options: It adds revenue but also generates varied and hazardous waste the system must absorb
Industry produces no waste, so there is no trade-off
Economic growth always improves environmental indicators
Hazardous waste is automatically recycled
optionExplanations: Correct: economic and environmental indicators can move in opposite directions — a real trade-off.
Wrong: industry is a major waste source.
Wrong: growth and environment can conflict.
Wrong: hazardous waste needs deliberate handling.

roundEvents.title: Wrap and Throw
roundEvents.brief: With no proper sewers or collection in the poorest districts, people wrap their waste in paper and throw it straight into the river — the "wrap and throw" habit. The water downstream is turning foul, and the wells nearby are no longer safe to drink from.
roundEvents.caseFact: In Manila, human waste is wrapped in newspaper and thrown into rivers — the "wrap and throw" method.

actions.label: Build basic sanitation and collection
actions.resultExplain: Fixes the real cause Expensive and takes time to build.
actions.plainMeaning: Give these districts toilets, sewers and rubbish pickup.
cons: Expensive and takes time to build.
actions.conceptLink: how dumping in water spreads pollution far downstream.

actions.label: Clean the river and run awareness drives
actions.resultExplain: Visible quick win The waste still needs somewhere proper to go.
actions.plainLabel: Clean the river and run awareness drives
actions.plainMeaning: Clear the waterway and teach people why dumping harms them.
cons: The waste still needs somewhere proper to go.
actions.conceptLink: how dumping in water spreads pollution far downstream.

actions.label: Tolerate it
actions.resultExplain: Costs nothing now. Wells and the sea get poisoned
actions.plainMeaning: Do nothing about the river dumping.
cons: Wells and the sea get poisoned
actions.conceptLink: how dumping in water spreads pollution far downstream.

justify.question: 'Wrap-and-throw' river dumping shows how local mismanagement becomes a regional problem because...
options: leachate and waste travel downstream, contaminating water sources and coasts far from the source
rivers neutralise all contaminants they carry
the impact is confined to the exact dumping point
it only affects local fish-market prices
optionExplanations: Correct: flowing water transports pollutants, spreading contamination across the catchment and to the sea.
Wrong: rivers transport, not neutralise, most contaminants.
Wrong: the whole point is that effects spread well beyond the dumping site.
Wrong: the harm is ecological and public-health, far broader than fish prices.

roundEvents.scene: With no proper sewers or collection in the poorest districts, people wrap their waste in paper and throw it straight into the river — the "wrap and throw" habit. The water downstream is turning foul, and the wells nearby are no longer safe to drink from.
roundEvents.conceptLink: how dumping in water spreads pollution far downstream.

easy.question: Why does throwing rubbish in a river harm faraway places?
options: It flows downstream and pollutes
It stays in one spot
Rivers clean all waste
It only affects fish prices
optionExplanations: Right: water carries pollution far away.
Wrong: it spreads, not stays.
Wrong: rivers carry, not clean, waste.
Wrong: harm is far broader than prices.

standard.question: 'Wrap-and-throw' river dumping shows how local mismanagement becomes a regional problem because...
options: leachate and waste travel downstream, contaminating water sources and coasts far from the source
rivers neutralise all contaminants they carry
the impact is confined to the exact dumping point
it only affects local fish-market prices
optionExplanations: Correct: flowing water transports pollutants, spreading contamination across the catchment and to the sea.
Wrong: rivers transport, not neutralise, most contaminants.
Wrong: the whole point is that effects spread well beyond the dumping site.
Wrong: the harm is ecological and public-health, far broader than fish prices.

hard.question: 'Wrap and throw' is best understood as a symptom of which underlying failure?
options: Absent sanitation and collection, leaving residents no managed alternative
Excessive recycling enthusiasm
Over-investment in landfills
A surplus of clean water
optionExplanations: Correct: it is a coping response to missing infrastructure.
Wrong: unrelated to recycling.
Wrong: the problem is too little infrastructure, not too much.
Wrong: water scarcity/quality is the consequence, not the cause.

roundEvents.title: Teach the Future
roundEvents.brief: Teachers want to bring waste and recycling into every classroom — turning kids into the city's keenest sorters and gentle nags at home. It won't show up in this year's numbers, but it could change the city's habits for a generation.
roundEvents.caseFact: Changing public attitudes — linking waste to climate and making sorting logical — underpins Copenhagen's success.

actions.label: City-wide schools programme
actions.resultExplain: Builds lasting recycling habits cheaply Slow to show in the metrics.
actions.plainMeaning: Teach every school about reducing and sorting waste.
cons: Slow to show in the metrics.
actions.conceptLink: attitudes and participation are what make recycling work.

actions.label: One-off advertising campaign
actions.resultExplain: Cheap Limited reach
actions.plainMeaning: Run a short media push on recycling.
actions.conceptLink: attitudes and participation are what make recycling work.

actions.label: Skip it
actions.resultExplain: No cost. Habits never shift
actions.plainMeaning: Don't invest in education.
actions.conceptLink: attitudes and participation are what make recycling work.

justify.question: Waste education yields little in the current year. On what grounds is the investment still defended?
options: Participation is the binding constraint on whether reduce/recycle systems actually work
Bins sort waste automatically regardless of behaviour
Education removes the need for any infrastructure
Public attitudes have no measurable effect on recycling outcomes
optionExplanations: Correct: even good infrastructure fails without behaviour change, so building participation is a long-term enabler.
Wrong: sorting depends on people, not the bins themselves.
Wrong: education complements but cannot replace collection and processing infrastructure.
Wrong: participation rates strongly determine real recycling performance.

roundEvents.scene: Teachers want to bring waste and recycling into every classroom — turning kids into the city's keenest sorters and gentle nags at home. It won't show up in this year's numbers, but it could change the city's habits for a generation.
roundEvents.conceptLink: attitudes and participation are what make recycling work.

easy.question: Why teach children about recycling?
options: It builds good habits for the future
Bins sort waste by themselves
It replaces all bins
Habits don't matter
optionExplanations: Right: participation depends on attitudes.
Wrong: bins don't sort themselves.
Wrong: education adds to, not replaces, infrastructure.
Wrong: habits strongly affect recycling.

standard.question: Waste education yields little in the current year. On what grounds is the investment still defended?
options: Participation is the binding constraint on whether reduce/recycle systems actually work
Bins sort waste automatically regardless of behaviour
Education removes the need for any infrastructure
Public attitudes have no measurable effect on recycling outcomes
optionExplanations: Correct: even good infrastructure fails without behaviour change, so building participation is a long-term enabler.
Wrong: sorting depends on people, not the bins themselves.
Wrong: education complements but cannot replace collection and processing infrastructure.
Wrong: participation rates strongly determine real recycling performance.

hard.question: Education spending yields no metric gain this year. On what basis is it still rational?
options: It builds future participation, the binding constraint on whether recovery systems succeed
It substitutes entirely for collection infrastructure
It produces immediate measurable recycling gains
Attitudes are irrelevant to system performance
optionExplanations: Correct: it is a long-term investment in the factor that limits recovery.
Wrong: it complements, not replaces, infrastructure.
Wrong: that contradicts the premise of a lag.
Wrong: attitudes strongly shape outcomes.

roundEvents.title: Digital Discards
roundEvents.brief: Old phones, laptops and chargers are piling up as people upgrade constantly. This "e-waste" is tricky — it's full of toxic metals, but also valuable materials worth recovering. Right now most of it is just getting tossed in with normal rubbish.
roundEvents.caseFact: Electronics rely on rare-earth and critical raw materials; e-waste contains toxic and valuable components.

actions.label: Set up e-waste take-back and recovery
actions.resultExplain: Recovers precious materials and income Costs to set up specialist handling.
actions.plainLabel: Set up e-waste take-back and recovery
actions.plainMeaning: Collect old electronics and recover the valuable metals inside.
pros: keeps toxins out of the ground.
cons: Costs to set up specialist handling.
actions.conceptLink: e-waste as both a hazard and a recoverable resource ("urban mining").

actions.label: Open a hazardous-waste drop-off
actions.resultExplain: Stops the worst contamination Recovers less of the value.
actions.plainMeaning: Give people a safe place to hand in dangerous items.
actions.conceptLink: e-waste as both a hazard and a recoverable resource ("urban mining").

actions.label: Mix it into normal rubbish
actions.resultExplain: Cheapest, no effort. Toxic metals leak into soil and water
actions.plainMeaning: Treat e-waste like any other waste.
cons: Toxic metals leak into soil and water
actions.conceptLink: e-waste as both a hazard and a recoverable resource ("urban mining").

justify.question: Why should e-waste be treated as a resource stream rather than ordinary rubbish?
options: It contains recoverable critical materials and hazardous substances that must be contained
It is biodegradable and harmless if buried
It cannot be recycled by any known method
It has no economic value
optionExplanations: Correct: e-waste holds valuable critical/rare-earth materials and toxics, so recovery captures value and prevents contamination.
Wrong: it is non-biodegradable and leaches heavy metals.
Wrong: established 'urban mining' processes recover its materials.
Wrong: the recoverable metals give it real economic value.

roundEvents.scene: Old phones, laptops and chargers are piling up as people upgrade constantly. This "e-waste" is tricky — it's full of toxic metals, but also valuable materials worth recovering. Right now most of it is just getting tossed in with normal rubbish.
roundEvents.conceptLink: e-waste as both a hazard and a recoverable resource ("urban mining").

easy.question: Why is old electronics ('e-waste') special?
options: It has valuable metals and toxic parts
It is food waste
It is harmless and rots away
It has no value
optionExplanations: Right: it holds recoverable metals and toxics.
Wrong: it isn't food waste.
Wrong: it's toxic and non-biodegradable.
Wrong: its metals have real value.

standard.question: Why should e-waste be treated as a resource stream rather than ordinary rubbish?
options: It contains recoverable critical materials and hazardous substances that must be contained
It is biodegradable and harmless if buried
It cannot be recycled by any known method
It has no economic value
optionExplanations: Correct: e-waste holds valuable critical/rare-earth materials and toxics, so recovery captures value and prevents contamination.
Wrong: it is non-biodegradable and leaches heavy metals.
Wrong: established 'urban mining' processes recover its materials.
Wrong: the recoverable metals give it real economic value.

hard.question: Why is e-waste described as simultaneously a hazard and an opportunity?
options: It contains toxic substances needing containment and critical materials worth recovering
It is biodegradable yet valuable
It is harmless but worthless
It is both impossible to recycle and toxic
optionExplanations: Correct: the dual nature — risk and resource — defines how it should be handled.
Wrong: it is not biodegradable.
Wrong: it is hazardous and valuable, not harmless/worthless.
Wrong: it is recyclable via urban mining.

roundEvents.title: The Last Cell
roundEvents.brief: The city's main rubbish tip is nearly full — engineers reckon it has less than a year left. Finding new land near a crowded city is hard and unpopular, and the trucks keep rolling in every day. You need a plan before the gates are forced shut.
roundEvents.caseFact: Cities are running out of landfill space; finding suitable land near dense urban areas is increasingly hard.

actions.label: Build a proper sealed landfill
actions.resultExplain: Buys safe disposal space Expensive
actions.plainMeaning: Construct a modern, lined tip that won't leak into the environment.
pros: far cleaner than an open tip.
cons: the space is still finite and will fill again.
actions.conceptLink: cities are running out of landfill space.

actions.label: Open a cheap open dump
actions.resultExplain: Instant relief Poisons land, air and water
actions.plainMeaning: Just start tipping rubbish on open ground.
actions.conceptLink: cities are running out of landfill space.

actions.label: Cut the rubbish coming in with recycling
actions.resultExplain: Tackles the real problem Slower
actions.plainLabel: Cut the rubbish coming in with recycling
actions.plainMeaning: Reduce how much waste needs burying in the first place.
actions.conceptLink: cities are running out of landfill space.

justify.question: Why is responding to a full landfill by simply opening another one regarded as an unsustainable 'trap' rather than a solution?
options: Land is finite and every new site reproduces lasting leachate, methane and land-opportunity costs
Landfills can be operated indefinitely at no environmental cost
Suitable land near cities is effectively unlimited
Landfills recycle the waste they receive
optionExplanations: Correct: it postpones the problem while consuming scarce land and locking in long-term pollution liabilities.
Wrong: landfills carry ongoing leachate, gas and monitoring costs.
Wrong: peri-urban land is scarce and contested, which is the core constraint.
Wrong: landfills store waste; they do not recycle it.

roundEvents.scene: The city's main rubbish tip is nearly full — engineers reckon it has less than a year left. Finding new land near a crowded city is hard and unpopular, and the trucks keep rolling in every day. You need a plan before the gates are forced shut.
roundEvents.conceptLink: cities are running out of landfill space.

easy.question: Why can't a city just keep building new tips?
options: Land runs out and tips pollute
Land is unlimited
Tips are free forever
Tips recycle the waste
optionExplanations: Right: land is finite and tips cause pollution.
Wrong: urban land is scarce.
Wrong: tips have ongoing costs.
Wrong: tips store, not recycle.

standard.question: Why is responding to a full landfill by simply opening another one regarded as an unsustainable 'trap' rather than a solution?
options: Land is finite and every new site reproduces lasting leachate, methane and land-opportunity costs
Landfills can be operated indefinitely at no environmental cost
Suitable land near cities is effectively unlimited
Landfills recycle the waste they receive
optionExplanations: Correct: it postpones the problem while consuming scarce land and locking in long-term pollution liabilities.
Wrong: landfills carry ongoing leachate, gas and monitoring costs.
Wrong: peri-urban land is scarce and contested, which is the core constraint.
Wrong: landfills store waste; they do not recycle it.

hard.question: Why is 'build another landfill' described as deferring rather than solving the problem?
options: It consumes finite land and creates long-lived liabilities without reducing waste generation
Landfills permanently neutralise the waste they receive
Land near cities is effectively abundant
It eliminates the need for collection
optionExplanations: Correct: it postpones the issue while adding liabilities and using scarce land.
Wrong: landfills store, not neutralise.
Wrong: peri-urban land is scarce.
Wrong: collection is still required.

roundEvents.title: The Cheap Option
roundEvents.brief: Budget hawks on the council are pushing hard for the cheapest fix: just dump it on open land and move on. It would save a fortune this year. But you've seen what open dumps do to the people who live near them.
roundEvents.caseFact: Over 90% of waste in low-income countries is openly dumped or burned, with serious consequences.

actions.label: Refuse — pay for a proper sealed tip
actions.resultExplain: Protects residents Strains the budget now.
actions.plainLabel: Refuse — pay for a proper sealed tip
actions.plainMeaning: Spend more to protect health and the environment.
actions.conceptLink: open dumping vs engineered disposal in poorer cities.

actions.label: Approve the open dump
actions.resultExplain: Big budget saving Severe pollution and health damage
actions.plainMeaning: Take the cheapest route.
cons: a problem that grows every year.
actions.conceptLink: open dumping vs engineered disposal in poorer cities.

actions.label: Compromise: controlled tipping with soil cover
actions.resultExplain: Cheaper than full engineering Still leaks and pollutes more than a sealed site.
actions.plainLabel: Compromise: controlled tipping with soil cover
actions.plainMeaning: A halfway option — cover the rubbish daily but skip full engineering.
cons: Still leaks and pollutes more than a sealed site.
actions.conceptLink: open dumping vs engineered disposal in poorer cities.

justify.question: What fundamentally distinguishes an engineered landfill from an open dump?
options: Containment engineering — lining, leachate treatment and gas control that limit contamination
Only the official name authorities give the site
Open dumps always cover a larger area
Engineered landfills simply accept more waste per day
optionExplanations: Correct: the defining difference is the engineered barriers and treatment that contain pollution.
Wrong: it is an engineering distinction, not a naming one.
Wrong: size is not the distinguishing feature.
Wrong: throughput is not what makes a site 'engineered'.

roundEvents.scene: Budget hawks on the council are pushing hard for the cheapest fix: just dump it on open land and move on. It would save a fortune this year. But you've seen what open dumps do to the people who live near them.
roundEvents.conceptLink: open dumping vs engineered disposal in poorer cities.

easy.question: What makes a proper landfill safer than an open dump?
options: Lining and controls that stop pollution leaking
Only its name
It is bigger
It takes more waste daily
optionExplanations: Right: engineering contains the pollution.
Wrong: it's not just a name.
Wrong: size isn't the point.
Wrong: throughput isn't the point.

standard.question: What fundamentally distinguishes an engineered landfill from an open dump?
options: Containment engineering — lining, leachate treatment and gas control that limit contamination
Only the official name authorities give the site
Open dumps always cover a larger area
Engineered landfills simply accept more waste per day
optionExplanations: Correct: the defining difference is the engineered barriers and treatment that contain pollution.
Wrong: it is an engineering distinction, not a naming one.
Wrong: size is not the distinguishing feature.
Wrong: throughput is not what makes a site 'engineered'.

hard.question: Evaluating the cheapest disposal route, what is the central hidden cost of an open dump?
options: Externalised health and environmental damage borne mainly by nearby poor communities
Higher upfront capital than engineered sites
Lower daily capacity than a sealed tip
Excessive recycling output
optionExplanations: Correct: the 'cheapness' relies on costs being shifted onto the vulnerable.
Wrong: open dumps are cheaper up front, not dearer.
Wrong: capacity is not the hidden cost.
Wrong: dumps do not boost recycling.

roundEvents.title: Island of Last Resort
roundEvents.brief: Engineers pitch something bold: a landfill built offshore, ringed with clay and waterproof lining, with its own plant to treat the toxic runoff so the sea stays clean. It's world-class — and it comes with a world-class price tag.
roundEvents.caseFact: Semakau (1999, SGD$610m) is lined with membrane, marine clay and rock; leachate is treated to keep waters clean.

actions.label: Fund the full sealed island tip
actions.resultExplain: Best protection for the sea and groundwater Heavy debt that drains future budgets.
actions.plainLabel: Fund the full sealed island tip
actions.plainMeaning: Build it properly, borrowing money to do so.
pros: Best protection for the sea and groundwater
cons: Heavy debt that drains future budgets.
actions.conceptLink: a well-engineered landfill (the Semakau model) and why lining matters.

actions.label: Build a cheaper unlined version
actions.resultExplain: Cheaper Toxic runoff threatens groundwater and the sea.
actions.plainMeaning: Skip the expensive lining to save money.
cons: Toxic runoff threatens groundwater and the sea.
actions.conceptLink: a well-engineered landfill (the Semakau model) and why lining matters.

actions.label: Reject it — push recycling instead
actions.resultExplain: No giant tip needed if you can reduce enough Risky if recycling can't keep up with the waste.
actions.plainMeaning: Avoid the landfill by cutting waste volume.
pros: No giant tip needed if you can reduce enough
cons: Risky if recycling can't keep up with the waste.
actions.conceptLink: a well-engineered landfill (the Semakau model) and why lining matters.

justify.question: Why is impermeable lining MOST critical when a landfill sits above or near an aquifer?
options: Leachate can percolate into groundwater that communities rely on for drinking water
Lining mainly makes the site look tidier
Lining speeds up decomposition of the waste
The presence of an aquifer is irrelevant to landfill design
optionExplanations: Correct: without a barrier, toxic leachate reaches the aquifer, directly threatening water supplies.
Wrong: appearance is not the function of lining.
Wrong: lining contains liquids; it does not accelerate decomposition.
Wrong: an underlying aquifer is precisely what makes containment essential.

roundEvents.scene: Engineers pitch something bold: a landfill built offshore, ringed with clay and waterproof lining, with its own plant to treat the toxic runoff so the sea stays clean. It's world-class — and it comes with a world-class price tag.
roundEvents.conceptLink: a well-engineered landfill (the Semakau model) and why lining matters.

easy.question: Why line the bottom of a landfill?
options: To stop toxic liquid reaching water
To make it look neat
To speed up rotting
No real reason
optionExplanations: Right: lining keeps leachate out of water.
Wrong: looks aren't the function.
Wrong: it contains liquid, not speeds decay.
Wrong: it has a clear purpose.

standard.question: Why is impermeable lining MOST critical when a landfill sits above or near an aquifer?
options: Leachate can percolate into groundwater that communities rely on for drinking water
Lining mainly makes the site look tidier
Lining speeds up decomposition of the waste
The presence of an aquifer is irrelevant to landfill design
optionExplanations: Correct: without a barrier, toxic leachate reaches the aquifer, directly threatening water supplies.
Wrong: appearance is not the function of lining.
Wrong: lining contains liquids; it does not accelerate decomposition.
Wrong: an underlying aquifer is precisely what makes containment essential.

hard.question: Why is full lining and leachate treatment most justified above an aquifer, despite the cost?
options: Contaminating a drinking-water aquifer is largely irreversible and high-consequence
Lining mainly improves the site's appearance
Aquifers accelerate waste decomposition
Cost is the only relevant factor
optionExplanations: Correct: irreversibility and high stakes justify the expense.
Wrong: appearance is not the rationale.
Wrong: aquifers do not speed decay.
Wrong: consequence, not just cost, drives the decision.

roundEvents.title: Not in Our Backyard, Either
roundEvents.brief: For years you've trucked your rubbish out to a rural town that quietly took it. Now that town has had enough — its people are blockading the road, sick of bearing your city's waste. The lorries are backing up.
roundEvents.caseFact: Collected waste often ends in landfills far from the city, creating "waste hinterlands" that bear the burden.

actions.label: Negotiate a fair deal with the town
actions.resultExplain: Keeps disposal flowing Costs money to compensate them.
actions.plainLabel: Negotiate a fair deal with the town
actions.plainMeaning: Share benefits and compensation so they'll keep helping.
actions.conceptLink: "waste hinterlands" — the countryside that carries the city's burden.

actions.label: Truck it even further away
actions.resultExplain: Solves it quickly without a fight. More fuel, more emissions, higher cost — and the same problem elsewhere.
actions.plainMeaning: Find a more distant site to dump in.
pros: Solves it quickly without a fight.
cons: More fuel, more emissions, higher cost — and the same problem elsewhere.
actions.conceptLink: "waste hinterlands" — the countryside that carries the city's burden.

actions.label: Send them less by recycling more
actions.resultExplain: Eases the pressure for good Costs money and takes time to ramp up.
actions.plainLabel: Send them less by recycling more
actions.plainMeaning: Shrink the waste you export.
cons: Costs money and takes time to ramp up.
actions.conceptLink: "waste hinterlands" — the countryside that carries the city's burden.

justify.question: Why is trucking waste to a distant rural 'hinterland' criticised as a false solution?
options: It displaces the environmental burden onto another community without reducing total waste
It permanently destroys the waste
It is always the cheapest option available
It eliminates transport emissions
optionExplanations: Correct: the waste and its impacts still exist — they are merely relocated onto people with less power.
Wrong: relocation is not destruction; the waste persists.
Wrong: long hauls add fuel and compensation costs.
Wrong: longer transport increases, not eliminates, emissions.

roundEvents.scene: For years you've trucked your rubbish out to a rural town that quietly took it. Now that town has had enough — its people are blockading the road, sick of bearing your city's waste. The lorries are backing up.
roundEvents.conceptLink: "waste hinterlands" — the countryside that carries the city's burden.

easy.question: Why isn't sending waste to a faraway town a real fix?
options: The waste and harm still exist, just elsewhere
It destroys the waste
It is always cheapest
Towns want the waste
optionExplanations: Right: it relocates the burden, not removes it.
Wrong: relocation isn't destruction.
Wrong: long hauls add cost.
Wrong: communities increasingly refuse it.

standard.question: Why is trucking waste to a distant rural 'hinterland' criticised as a false solution?
options: It displaces the environmental burden onto another community without reducing total waste
It permanently destroys the waste
It is always the cheapest option available
It eliminates transport emissions
optionExplanations: Correct: the waste and its impacts still exist — they are merely relocated onto people with less power.
Wrong: relocation is not destruction; the waste persists.
Wrong: long hauls add fuel and compensation costs.
Wrong: longer transport increases, not eliminates, emissions.

hard.question: The 'waste hinterland' concept is best used to challenge which assumption?
options: That waste exported out of sight has genuinely been dealt with
That recycling is unprofitable
That cities have no rural links
That transport is emission-free
optionExplanations: Correct: it exposes 'out of sight, out of mind' as a false resolution.
Wrong: not its central point.
Wrong: it assumes such links exist.
Wrong: a secondary issue, not the core assumption.

roundEvents.title: Poison in the Water
roundEvents.brief: Tests come back grim: a dark, toxic liquid is seeping out of your rubbish site and creeping toward the wells families drink from. Doctors are warning of risks to pregnant women and babies. The clock is ticking.
roundEvents.caseFact: Studies link living near landfills to raised risks of birth defects from contaminated water and soil.

actions.label: Build a runoff treatment plant
actions.resultExplain: Stops the contamination directly Expensive to build and run.
actions.plainMeaning: Capture and clean the toxic liquid before it spreads.
actions.conceptLink: leachate — the poisonous liquid that drips from rotting waste.

actions.label: Hand out bottled water for now
actions.resultExplain: Cheap Doesn't fix anything
actions.plainLabel: Hand out bottled water for now
actions.plainMeaning: A stopgap while you decide what to do.
actions.conceptLink: leachate — the poisonous liquid that drips from rotting waste.

actions.label: Deny it and just monitor
actions.resultExplain: Costs almost nothing now. People get sick
actions.plainMeaning: Downplay the problem and watch it.
actions.conceptLink: leachate — the poisonous liquid that drips from rotting waste.

justify.question: Which definition of 'leachate' is correct, and why is it hazardous?
options: Liquid that percolates through waste, carrying dissolved contaminants and heavy metals into soil and water
Recycled water produced by a treatment plant
A gaseous form of methane released by landfills
Clean rainwater runoff from a capped site
optionExplanations: Correct: rainwater passing through waste dissolves toxins, which then migrate into soil and groundwater.
Wrong: leachate is contaminated, not treated, water.
Wrong: leachate is a liquid; methane is the gaseous emission.
Wrong: it is contaminated, not clean, runoff.

roundEvents.scene: Tests come back grim: a dark, toxic liquid is seeping out of your rubbish site and creeping toward the wells families drink from. Doctors are warning of risks to pregnant women and babies. The clock is ticking.
roundEvents.conceptLink: leachate — the poisonous liquid that drips from rotting waste.

easy.question: What is 'leachate'?
options: Toxic liquid that drips from rotting rubbish
Clean drinking water
A type of gas
Recycled water
optionExplanations: Right: it's contaminated liquid from waste.
Wrong: it's polluted, not clean.
Wrong: that's the gas (methane).
Wrong: it isn't treated water.

standard.question: Which definition of 'leachate' is correct, and why is it hazardous?
options: Liquid that percolates through waste, carrying dissolved contaminants and heavy metals into soil and water
Recycled water produced by a treatment plant
A gaseous form of methane released by landfills
Clean rainwater runoff from a capped site
optionExplanations: Correct: rainwater passing through waste dissolves toxins, which then migrate into soil and groundwater.
Wrong: leachate is contaminated, not treated, water.
Wrong: leachate is a liquid; methane is the gaseous emission.
Wrong: it is contaminated, not clean, runoff.

hard.question: Why is leachate a particularly insidious pollutant?
options: It silently migrates through soil into groundwater, with health effects appearing only later
It is immediately visible and easily contained
It evaporates harmlessly
It only affects surface aesthetics
optionExplanations: Correct: delayed, hidden contamination makes it hard to detect and address in time.
Wrong: it is often invisible and mobile.
Wrong: it persists in soil and water.
Wrong: it is a serious health hazard.

roundEvents.title: The Invisible Threat
roundEvents.brief: Deep in the rubbish mound, rotting food is releasing methane — an invisible gas that's both explosive and a powerful driver of climate change. Workers can smell it. One spark and the whole tip could catch fire for months.
roundEvents.caseFact: Landfills cause ~11% of global methane emissions; methane pockets at Deonar sparked a 3-month fire.

actions.label: Capture the gas and turn it into power
actions.resultExplain: Turns a dangerous gas into useful energy and income Costs money to install the system.
actions.plainLabel: Capture the gas and turn it into power
actions.plainMeaning: Trap the methane and burn it for electricity.
pros: Turns a dangerous gas into useful energy and income
cons: Costs money to install the system.
actions.conceptLink: buried organic waste makes methane, a potent greenhouse gas.

actions.label: Safely flare it off
actions.resultExplain: Removes the explosion risk Wastes the energy
actions.plainMeaning: Burn the gas off in a controlled way.
pros: less harmful than letting it escape.
actions.conceptLink: buried organic waste makes methane, a potent greenhouse gas.

actions.label: Leave it
actions.resultExplain: No spend. Fire risk and powerful warming emissions remain.
actions.plainMeaning: Do nothing about the gas.
cons: Fire risk and powerful warming emissions remain.
actions.conceptLink: buried organic waste makes methane, a potent greenhouse gas.

justify.question: Why is uncaptured landfill methane a particular climate priority compared with the CO2 from the same waste?
options: Methane has a far higher short-term global-warming potential than CO2
Methane cools the atmosphere
Landfills emit no methane
Methane becomes harmless immediately on release
optionExplanations: Correct: per tonne, methane traps far more heat over the short term, so capturing it yields outsized climate benefit.
Wrong: methane is a potent warming gas, not a coolant.
Wrong: anaerobic decay in landfills is a major methane source.
Wrong: it remains a powerful greenhouse gas once emitted.

roundEvents.scene: Deep in the rubbish mound, rotting food is releasing methane — an invisible gas that's both explosive and a powerful driver of climate change. Workers can smell it. One spark and the whole tip could catch fire for months.
roundEvents.conceptLink: buried organic waste makes methane, a potent greenhouse gas.

easy.question: Why is the gas from rotting rubbish a problem?
options: It warms the planet and can catch fire
It cools the air
It is harmless
Rubbish makes no gas
optionExplanations: Right: methane is flammable and warms the climate.
Wrong: it warms, not cools.
Wrong: it's hazardous.
Wrong: rotting waste makes lots of gas.

standard.question: Why is uncaptured landfill methane a particular climate priority compared with the CO2 from the same waste?
options: Methane has a far higher short-term global-warming potential than CO2
Methane cools the atmosphere
Landfills emit no methane
Methane becomes harmless immediately on release
optionExplanations: Correct: per tonne, methane traps far more heat over the short term, so capturing it yields outsized climate benefit.
Wrong: methane is a potent warming gas, not a coolant.
Wrong: anaerobic decay in landfills is a major methane source.
Wrong: it remains a powerful greenhouse gas once emitted.

hard.question: Capturing landfill methane is often prioritised over flaring. Why?
options: Capture both prevents potent emissions and recovers usable energy
Flaring produces more energy than capture
Methane has no climate effect
Capture increases explosion risk
optionExplanations: Correct: capture delivers a climate and an energy benefit at once.
Wrong: flaring wastes the energy.
Wrong: methane is a potent GHG.
Wrong: capture reduces, not raises, risk.

roundEvents.title: The Long Haul
roundEvents.brief: Your rubbish trucks are driving further and further to reach disposal sites, guzzling fuel and clogging roads. The fuel bill is ballooning and so are the exhaust fumes. Something has to give.
roundEvents.caseFact: Fuel to transport waste over longer distances adds to a city's ecological footprint.

actions.label: Build a local sorting/transfer station
actions.resultExplain: Cuts driving and fuel Upfront building cost.
actions.plainMeaning: Set up a nearby hub so trucks travel less and materials get recovered.
actions.conceptLink: hauling waste long distances adds cost and emissions to the footprint.

actions.label: Use smart route planning
actions.resultExplain: Saves fuel and money with little disruption. Doesn't reduce the underlying waste volume.
actions.plainMeaning: Optimise truck routes with technology.
pros: Saves fuel and money with little disruption.
cons: Doesn't reduce the underlying waste volume.
actions.conceptLink: hauling waste long distances adds cost and emissions to the footprint.

actions.label: Accept the rising costs
actions.resultExplain: No upfront spend. Bleeds budget and emissions every year.
actions.plainMeaning: Keep hauling as is.
cons: Bleeds budget and emissions every year.
actions.conceptLink: hauling waste long distances adds cost and emissions to the footprint.

justify.question: Why does the distance waste is hauled matter for sustainability, not just for the budget?
options: Longer hauls burn more fuel, adding emissions to the waste system's ecological footprint
Hauling distance has no environmental consequence
Longer transport raises recycling rates
Collection vehicles produce no emissions
optionExplanations: Correct: transport fuel use is part of the system's footprint, so distance has a real environmental cost.
Wrong: fuel combustion makes distance environmentally significant.
Wrong: distance does not improve recycling.
Wrong: diesel collection fleets are a notable emissions source.

roundEvents.scene: Your rubbish trucks are driving further and further to reach disposal sites, guzzling fuel and clogging roads. The fuel bill is ballooning and so are the exhaust fumes. Something has to give.
roundEvents.conceptLink: hauling waste long distances adds cost and emissions to the footprint.

easy.question: Why does driving rubbish far away matter?
options: It burns more fuel and pollutes more
Distance doesn't matter
It boosts recycling
Trucks have no exhaust
optionExplanations: Right: more fuel means more emissions and cost.
Wrong: distance adds real cost.
Wrong: it doesn't help recycling.
Wrong: trucks emit exhaust.

standard.question: Why does the distance waste is hauled matter for sustainability, not just for the budget?
options: Longer hauls burn more fuel, adding emissions to the waste system's ecological footprint
Hauling distance has no environmental consequence
Longer transport raises recycling rates
Collection vehicles produce no emissions
optionExplanations: Correct: transport fuel use is part of the system's footprint, so distance has a real environmental cost.
Wrong: fuel combustion makes distance environmentally significant.
Wrong: distance does not improve recycling.
Wrong: diesel collection fleets are a notable emissions source.

hard.question: Why does long-distance hauling weaken the case for centralised disposal?
options: Transport fuel adds emissions and cost that scale with distance, enlarging the footprint
Distance has no bearing on emissions
Centralisation always lowers the footprint
Hauling improves recycling quality
optionExplanations: Correct: haul distance is a real environmental and financial cost of centralisation.
Wrong: fuel use scales with distance.
Wrong: centralisation can raise transport impacts.
Wrong: hauling does not improve recycling.

roundEvents.title: Whose Land?
roundEvents.brief: A closed-off chunk of land beside the old tip has become a tug-of-war: developers want it for housing, locals want a park, and your team wants to keep it as a safety buffer. Everyone's lobbying you hard.
roundEvents.caseFact: Proximity of landfills to residential areas creates land-use conflicts in dense cities.

actions.label: Turn the capped tip into a park
actions.resultExplain: Old tips can become parks and pitches Costs money
actions.plainLabel: Turn the capped tip into a park
actions.plainMeaning: Seal the old site safely and open green space on top.
pros: Old tips can become parks and pitches
actions.conceptLink: land-use conflict around waste sites in crowded cities.

actions.label: Sell it to developers
actions.resultExplain: Quick money for the city. Homes near old contamination
actions.plainMeaning: Cash in by allowing housing.
actions.conceptLink: land-use conflict around waste sites in crowded cities.

actions.label: Keep it as a buffer
actions.resultExplain: Safe and cautious. Misses out on both money and amenity.
actions.plainMeaning: Leave the land empty for safety.
cons: Misses out on both money and amenity.
actions.conceptLink: land-use conflict around waste sites in crowded cities.

justify.question: How can decommissioned landfill land be returned to safe public use?
options: A properly sealed and capped site, once stabilised, can host parks or playing fields
Such land can never be reused for any purpose
It is only ever safe for dense housing
It must immediately be reopened as a new dump
optionExplanations: Correct: with capping and gas/leachate management, former landfills are commonly converted to green amenity.
Wrong: managed reuse is well established, so 'never' is false.
Wrong: housing directly on a former tip raises contamination risk; open amenity is safer.
Wrong: reuse aims to retire the site, not perpetuate dumping.

roundEvents.scene: A closed-off chunk of land beside the old tip has become a tug-of-war: developers want it for housing, locals want a park, and your team wants to keep it as a safety buffer. Everyone's lobbying you hard.
roundEvents.conceptLink: land-use conflict around waste sites in crowded cities.

easy.question: What can an old, sealed tip become?
options: A park or playing field
Never anything
Only dense housing
A new dump
optionExplanations: Right: capped sites become green amenity.
Wrong: reuse is common.
Wrong: housing on a tip is risky.
Wrong: reuse retires the dump.

standard.question: How can decommissioned landfill land be returned to safe public use?
options: A properly sealed and capped site, once stabilised, can host parks or playing fields
Such land can never be reused for any purpose
It is only ever safe for dense housing
It must immediately be reopened as a new dump
optionExplanations: Correct: with capping and gas/leachate management, former landfills are commonly converted to green amenity.
Wrong: managed reuse is well established, so 'never' is false.
Wrong: housing directly on a former tip raises contamination risk; open amenity is safer.
Wrong: reuse aims to retire the site, not perpetuate dumping.

hard.question: What condition makes converting a former landfill into parkland safe?
options: Proper capping plus ongoing gas and leachate management once the site has stabilised
Immediate construction before stabilisation
Removing all monitoring systems
Building housing directly on the waste
