A pledge to ban consumption
Expanded landfill capacity
optionExplanations: Correct: enforceable substance, not messaging, makes it credible.
Wrong: branding is the greenwashing risk.
Wrong: it targets throughput, not consumption bans.
Wrong: more landfill contradicts the goal.

roundEvents.title: Industrial Symbiosis
roundEvents.brief: Your factories could be linked so that one company's waste becomes another's raw material — a clever web where almost nothing is thrown away. It takes coordination, but it could make your industry world-leading in circularity.
roundEvents.caseFact: "Technical products" can circulate continuously as materials in the industrial cycle.

actions.label: Build an eco-industrial park
actions.resultExplain: Creates closed material loops and local value Needs coordination and investment.
actions.plainMeaning: Cluster industries so they swap and reuse each other's by-products.
pros: Creates closed material loops and local value
actions.conceptLink: industrial symbiosis and the technical materials cycle.

actions.label: Set up a by-product exchange platform
actions.resultExplain: Lower-cost matchmaking for waste-as-resource. Less impact than a full park.
actions.plainLabel: Set up a by-product exchange platform
actions.plainMeaning: A lighter system matching waste to firms that can use it.
cons: Less impact than a full park.
actions.conceptLink: industrial symbiosis and the technical materials cycle.

actions.label: Keep the status quo
actions.resultExplain: No spend. Industrial materials stay wasteful.
actions.plainMeaning: Leave industry as is.
actions.conceptLink: industrial symbiosis and the technical materials cycle.

justify.question: What does 'industrial symbiosis' mean within a circular economy?
options: One firm's waste or by-product becomes another firm's raw material
Firms competing to extract the most virgin resources
Burning all industrial waste for energy
Exporting industrial waste to other countries
optionExplanations: Correct: it closes loops between firms, turning outputs into inputs.
Wrong: that is linear competition for virgin material.
Wrong: combustion is recovery, not symbiosis.
Wrong: exporting waste is displacement, not symbiosis.

roundEvents.scene: Your factories could be linked so that one company's waste becomes another's raw material — a clever web where almost nothing is thrown away. It takes coordination, but it could make your industry world-leading in circularity.
roundEvents.conceptLink: industrial symbiosis and the technical materials cycle.

easy.question: What is 'industrial symbiosis'?
options: One firm's waste is another's raw material
Firms competing for resources
Burning all waste
Exporting waste
optionExplanations: Right: outputs become inputs between firms.
Wrong: that's linear competition.
Wrong: burning is recovery, not symbiosis.
Wrong: exporting is displacement.

standard.question: What does 'industrial symbiosis' mean within a circular economy?
options: One firm's waste or by-product becomes another firm's raw material
Firms competing to extract the most virgin resources
Burning all industrial waste for energy
Exporting industrial waste to other countries
optionExplanations: Correct: it closes loops between firms, turning outputs into inputs.
Wrong: that is linear competition for virgin material.
Wrong: combustion is recovery, not symbiosis.
Wrong: exporting waste is displacement, not symbiosis.

hard.question: Industrial symbiosis advances circularity because it...
options: turns one firm's by-products into another's feedstock, closing technical loops
intensifies competition for virgin inputs
relies on incinerating industrial waste
depends on exporting waste abroad
optionExplanations: Correct: it cycles materials between firms rather than discarding them.
Wrong: that is linear competition.
Wrong: combustion is not symbiosis.
Wrong: export is displacement.

roundEvents.title: Return and Refund
roundEvents.brief: A deposit-return law would add a small refundable charge to every bottle and can — pay it at purchase, get it back when you return the empty. Where it's been tried, return rates have soared.
roundEvents.caseFact: Deposit-return drives high reuse/recycling rates where collection works.

actions.label: Pass a full deposit-return law
actions.resultExplain: Proven, popular, high return rates Needs collection infrastructure.
actions.plainMeaning: Add refundable deposits to drinks containers citywide.
actions.conceptLink: deposit-return schemes driving reuse and recycling.

actions.label: Pilot it in one district
actions.resultExplain: Low-risk trial. Limited reach.
actions.plainMeaning: Test the scheme small-scale first.
actions.conceptLink: deposit-return schemes driving reuse and recycling.

actions.label: Rely on voluntary recycling
actions.resultExplain: No new system. Return rates stay low.
actions.plainMeaning: Skip the deposit.
actions.conceptLink: deposit-return schemes driving reuse and recycling.

justify.question: Why do container-deposit schemes achieve high return rates?
options: A refundable deposit gives a direct financial incentive to return the container
Deposits are punitive fines for buying drinks
They ban the sale of containers outright
Consumers ignore deposits entirely
optionExplanations: Correct: people return items to reclaim their money, so capture rates rise.
Wrong: it is a refundable deposit, not a fine.
Wrong: sales continue; only a deposit is added.
Wrong: the monetary incentive ensures most people do respond.

roundEvents.scene: A deposit-return law would add a small refundable charge to every bottle and can — pay it at purchase, get it back when you return the empty. Where it's been tried, return rates have soared.
roundEvents.conceptLink: deposit-return schemes driving reuse and recycling.

easy.question: Why do bottle deposits boost returns?
options: People return them to get money back
Deposits are fines
Bottles are banned
People ignore them
optionExplanations: Right: a refund is a direct incentive.
Wrong: it's refundable, not a fine.
Wrong: sales continue.
Wrong: most people respond to the refund.

standard.question: Why do container-deposit schemes achieve high return rates?
options: A refundable deposit gives a direct financial incentive to return the container
Deposits are punitive fines for buying drinks
They ban the sale of containers outright
Consumers ignore deposits entirely
optionExplanations: Correct: people return items to reclaim their money, so capture rates rise.
Wrong: it is a refundable deposit, not a fine.
Wrong: sales continue; only a deposit is added.
Wrong: the monetary incentive ensures most people do respond.

hard.question: Deposit-return schemes achieve high recovery because they...
options: attach a refundable financial incentive to returning containers
impose punitive fines on purchases
prohibit container sales
assume altruism without any incentive
optionExplanations: Correct: the refund directly motivates return.
Wrong: it is refundable, not a fine.
Wrong: sales continue.
Wrong: it relies on incentive, not altruism.

roundEvents.title: Close the Hole
roundEvents.brief: You can ban dumping anything that could be recycled or burned instead — forcing the city to choose better options. It only works because you've built the alternatives; now you can shut the easy escape hatch.
roundEvents.caseFact: Denmark made it illegal to landfill waste that can be incinerated.

actions.label: Ban landfilling of recyclables and combustibles
actions.resultExplain: Forces waste up the hierarchy Only works if you've built the alternatives.
actions.plainLabel: Ban landfilling of recyclables and combustibles
actions.plainMeaning: Forbid dumping anything that can be recovered.
cons: Only works if you've built the alternatives.
actions.conceptLink: banning landfilling of recyclable/combustible waste.

actions.label: Phase the ban in by material
actions.resultExplain: A smoother, gradual transition. Slower to take effect.
actions.plainLabel: Phase the ban in by material
actions.plainMeaning: Ban materials one at a time.
actions.conceptLink: banning landfilling of recyclable/combustible waste.

actions.label: No ban
actions.resultExplain: Keeps the easy option open. Circular progress stalls.
actions.plainMeaning: Keep landfilling allowed.
actions.conceptLink: banning landfilling of recyclable/combustible waste.

justify.question: Why is banning the landfilling of recyclable/combustible waste only effective once alternatives exist?
options: A ban forces waste up the hierarchy, but only works if recovery capacity can absorb the diverted material
Landfill is the ideal destination for such waste
Bans always increase the total waste generated
Bans are universally ineffective
optionExplanations: Correct: without recycling/recovery in place, a ban creates a disposal gap rather than progress.
Wrong: the ban exists precisely to move waste off landfill.
Wrong: a ban targets destination, not generation.
Wrong: paired with alternatives, bans are effective.

roundEvents.scene: You can ban dumping anything that could be recycled or burned instead — forcing the city to choose better options. It only works because you've built the alternatives; now you can shut the easy escape hatch.
roundEvents.conceptLink: banning landfilling of recyclable/combustible waste.

easy.question: Why ban dumping recyclables only when alternatives exist?
options: Waste needs somewhere better to go first
Landfill is best for them
Bans raise waste
Bans never work
optionExplanations: Right: without recovery in place, a ban creates a gap.
Wrong: the ban moves waste off landfill.
Wrong: a ban targets destination.
Wrong: with alternatives, bans work.

standard.question: Why is banning the landfilling of recyclable/combustible waste only effective once alternatives exist?
options: A ban forces waste up the hierarchy, but only works if recovery capacity can absorb the diverted material
Landfill is the ideal destination for such waste
Bans always increase the total waste generated
Bans are universally ineffective
optionExplanations: Correct: without recycling/recovery in place, a ban creates a disposal gap rather than progress.
Wrong: the ban exists precisely to move waste off landfill.
Wrong: a ban targets destination, not generation.
Wrong: paired with alternatives, bans are effective.

hard.question: A landfill ban on recyclables is only effective if...
options: sufficient recovery and treatment capacity exists to absorb diverted waste
landfill remains the preferred destination
no alternatives are developed
it is applied before any recycling exists
optionExplanations: Correct: without capacity to absorb diverted waste, a ban creates a disposal gap.
Wrong: the ban moves waste off landfill.
Wrong: alternatives are essential.
Wrong: premature bans fail.

roundEvents.title: The Circular Workforce
roundEvents.brief: A circular economy needs people who can repair, sort, recover and remanufacture — so you can invest in training a whole new green workforce. It's a chance to make sustainability a source of good jobs.
roundEvents.caseFact: Circular activities (repair, recovery, remanufacture) create local employment.

actions.label: Launch a city-wide green-skills programme
actions.resultExplain: Builds the workforce circularity needs Training costs.
actions.plainMeaning: Train residents for repair, recycling and remanufacturing jobs.
actions.conceptLink: the jobs and skills a circular economy creates.

actions.label: Subsidise circular start-ups
actions.resultExplain: Seeds innovation. Narrower than broad training.
actions.plainMeaning: Fund new businesses in the circular economy.
actions.conceptLink: the jobs and skills a circular economy creates.

actions.label: No programme
actions.resultExplain: No spend. The transition lacks skilled workers.
actions.plainMeaning: Don't invest in skills.
actions.conceptLink: the jobs and skills a circular economy creates.

justify.question: Why does a circular economy require investment in the workforce?
options: Repair, recovery and remanufacturing are labour-intensive activities that need new skills
Circular systems operate entirely without people
Employment is irrelevant to circularity
A circular economy necessarily reduces total employment
optionExplanations: Correct: circular activities create skilled jobs, so workforce development underpins the transition.
Wrong: these activities are people-intensive.
Wrong: labour is central to circular operations.
Wrong: circular sectors tend to add, not cut, jobs.

roundEvents.scene: A circular economy needs people who can repair, sort, recover and remanufacture — so you can invest in training a whole new green workforce. It's a chance to make sustainability a source of good jobs.
roundEvents.conceptLink: the jobs and skills a circular economy creates.

easy.question: Why does a circular economy need trained workers?
options: Repair and recycling are people-heavy jobs
It needs no people
Jobs don't matter
It cuts all jobs
optionExplanations: Right: circular work is labour-intensive.
Wrong: it needs people.
Wrong: labour is central.
Wrong: it adds jobs.

standard.question: Why does a circular economy require investment in the workforce?
options: Repair, recovery and remanufacturing are labour-intensive activities that need new skills
Circular systems operate entirely without people
Employment is irrelevant to circularity
A circular economy necessarily reduces total employment
optionExplanations: Correct: circular activities create skilled jobs, so workforce development underpins the transition.
Wrong: these activities are people-intensive.
Wrong: labour is central to circular operations.
Wrong: circular sectors tend to add, not cut, jobs.

hard.question: Why is workforce development integral to a circular transition?
options: Repair, recovery and remanufacturing are labour- and skill-intensive activities
Circular systems are fully automated
Employment is irrelevant to circularity
Circularity reduces total employment
optionExplanations: Correct: circular sectors need skilled people, so training underpins the shift.
Wrong: they are people-intensive.
Wrong: labour is central.
Wrong: circularity tends to add jobs.

roundEvents.title: Make Makers Pay
roundEvents.brief: Why should the city foot the bill for disposing of products companies sell? An "extended producer responsibility" law would make manufacturers pay to handle their goods at end of life — and design them to be recyclable in the first place.
roundEvents.caseFact: Polluter-Pays logic extends to producers funding the disposal of what they sell.

actions.label: Bring in full producer responsibility
actions.resultExplain: Shifts cost and design incentives onto producers Industry friction.
actions.plainMeaning: Make manufacturers fund the end-of-life of what they sell.
pros: Shifts cost and design incentives onto producers
actions.conceptLink: extended producer responsibility (EPR).

actions.label: Apply it to packaging and electronics only
actions.resultExplain: Tackles the biggest problems. Narrower scope.
actions.plainLabel: Apply it to packaging and electronics only
actions.plainMeaning: Target the worst waste streams first.
actions.conceptLink: extended producer responsibility (EPR).

actions.label: Decline
actions.resultExplain: Avoids industry fights. The city keeps paying the bill.
actions.plainMeaning: Don't introduce EPR.
cons: The city keeps paying the bill.
actions.conceptLink: extended producer responsibility (EPR).

justify.question: What behavioural change does Extended Producer Responsibility (EPR) aim to drive?
options: Making producers fund end-of-life handling incentivises durable, recyclable product design
Shifting all disposal costs onto consumers only
Banning the production of goods entirely
It has no effect on how products are designed
optionExplanations: Correct: when makers pay for disposal, they have a reason to design for recyclability and longevity.
Wrong: EPR places responsibility on producers, not solely consumers.
Wrong: it reshapes design and cost, not production itself.
Wrong: internalising end-of-life cost is specifically intended to change design.

roundEvents.scene: Why should the city foot the bill for disposing of products companies sell? An "extended producer responsibility" law would make manufacturers pay to handle their goods at end of life — and design them to be recyclable in the first place.
roundEvents.conceptLink: extended producer responsibility (EPR).

easy.question: What does making producers pay for disposal encourage?
options: Designing recyclable, longer-lasting products
Consumers paying only
Banning all goods
No design change
optionExplanations: Right: paying for end-of-life drives better design.
Wrong: it shifts cost to producers.
Wrong: it reshapes design, not bans goods.
Wrong: it changes design incentives.

standard.question: What behavioural change does Extended Producer Responsibility (EPR) aim to drive?
options: Making producers fund end-of-life handling incentivises durable, recyclable product design
Shifting all disposal costs onto consumers only
Banning the production of goods entirely
It has no effect on how products are designed
optionExplanations: Correct: when makers pay for disposal, they have a reason to design for recyclability and longevity.
Wrong: EPR places responsibility on producers, not solely consumers.
Wrong: it reshapes design and cost, not production itself.
Wrong: internalising end-of-life cost is specifically intended to change design.

hard.question: EPR shifts incentives by...
options: making producers bear end-of-life costs, rewarding recyclable, durable design
transferring all costs to consumers
prohibiting manufacturing
leaving product design unchanged
optionExplanations: Correct: internalising disposal cost drives better design.
Wrong: it places cost on producers.
Wrong: it reshapes design, not bans production.
Wrong: it specifically changes design incentives.

roundEvents.title: Putting It to the People
roundEvents.brief: You decide to put a new waste levy to a public vote — a real test of whether the city trusts you. If you've earned their faith over the years, they'll back it; if not, this could backfire.
roundEvents.caseFact: Public attitudes and buy-in are decisive for waste policy success.

actions.label: Campaign honestly for the levy
actions.resultExplain: Wins lasting public consent Risky if trust is already low.
actions.plainMeaning: Make the open, transparent case to voters.
cons: Risky if trust is already low.
actions.conceptLink: public trust and participation in waste policy.

actions.label: Water it down to ensure it passes
actions.resultExplain: Likely to pass. Weaker measure, smaller impact.
actions.plainLabel: Water it down to ensure it passes
actions.plainMeaning: Offer a weaker version voters will accept.
actions.conceptLink: public trust and participation in waste policy.

actions.label: Skip the vote and impose it
actions.resultExplain: Fast. Deepens distrust and future resistance.
actions.plainLabel: Skip the vote and impose it
actions.plainMeaning: Bring in the levy without asking.
actions.conceptLink: public trust and participation in waste policy.

justify.question: Why does the level of public trust shape which waste policies are politically feasible?
options: Citizens must consent to and cooperate with schemes for them to function
Effective policy can simply ignore public opinion
Higher trust reduces recycling participation
Public votes have no bearing on implementation
optionExplanations: Correct: waste policy relies on compliance and participation, which depend on trust.
Wrong: ignoring opinion invites resistance and non-compliance.
Wrong: trust supports participation.
Wrong: democratic consent strongly affects what can be enacted.

roundEvents.scene: You decide to put a new waste levy to a public vote — a real test of whether the city trusts you. If you've earned their faith over the years, they'll back it; if not, this could backfire.
roundEvents.conceptLink: public trust and participation in waste policy.

easy.question: Why does public trust shape waste policy?
options: People must accept and join schemes
Policy ignores people
Trust lowers recycling
Votes don't matter
optionExplanations: Right: compliance and participation need trust.
Wrong: ignoring opinion breeds resistance.
Wrong: trust supports participation.
Wrong: consent affects what's possible.

standard.question: Why does the level of public trust shape which waste policies are politically feasible?
options: Citizens must consent to and cooperate with schemes for them to function
Effective policy can simply ignore public opinion
Higher trust reduces recycling participation
Public votes have no bearing on implementation
optionExplanations: Correct: waste policy relies on compliance and participation, which depend on trust.
Wrong: ignoring opinion invites resistance and non-compliance.
Wrong: trust supports participation.
Wrong: democratic consent strongly affects what can be enacted.

hard.question: Public trust determines policy feasibility because...
options: waste schemes depend on voluntary compliance and participation
effective policy disregards public opinion
trust suppresses recycling
referendums cannot affect implementation
optionExplanations: Correct: schemes only work if citizens consent and take part.
Wrong: ignoring opinion breeds non-compliance.
Wrong: trust supports recycling.
Wrong: consent strongly shapes what is enacted.

roundEvents.title: Pay As You Throw
roundEvents.brief: New technology lets you charge households by exactly how much rubbish they throw out, with smart bins tracking it. People who waste less pay less — a direct, personal incentive to cut down. You just have to keep it fair.
roundEvents.caseFact: Pricing waste by volume creates direct incentives to reduce and sort.

actions.label: Roll out smart bins and pay-as-you-throw
actions.resultExplain: Strong personal incentive to reduce and sort Must be designed carefully so it's fair to poorer households.
actions.plainLabel: Roll out smart bins and pay-as-you-throw
actions.plainMeaning: Charge each household by how much waste they produce.
pros: Strong personal incentive to reduce and sort
cons: Must be designed carefully so it's fair to poorer households.
actions.conceptLink: pay-as-you-throw pricing and smart waste tech.

actions.label: Use smart bins for monitoring only
actions.resultExplain: Improves logistics. Doesn't change behaviour much.
actions.plainLabel: Use smart bins for monitoring only
actions.plainMeaning: Track waste without charging by volume.
actions.conceptLink: pay-as-you-throw pricing and smart waste tech.

actions.label: No new tech
actions.resultExplain: No spend. Misses the data and incentive gains.
actions.plainMeaning: Carry on without it.
cons: Misses the data and incentive gains.
actions.conceptLink: pay-as-you-throw pricing and smart waste tech.

justify.question: Why does 'pay-as-you-throw' cut household waste, and what design risk must be managed?
options: Charging by volume incentivises reduction and sorting, but it must stay fair to poorer households
It is free, so it changes nothing
It works by banning the use of bins
It increases the amount households throw away
optionExplanations: Correct: volume-based charging rewards waste reduction, but equity must be designed in to avoid unfair burdens.
Wrong: it is a pricing mechanism, not free.
Wrong: it prices waste; it does not ban bins.
Wrong: it reduces, not increases, household waste.

roundEvents.scene: New technology lets you charge households by exactly how much rubbish they throw out, with smart bins tracking it. People who waste less pay less — a direct, personal incentive to cut down. You just have to keep it fair.
roundEvents.conceptLink: pay-as-you-throw pricing and smart waste tech.

easy.question: Why does 'pay for what you throw' cut waste?
options: People waste less to pay less
It's free
It bans bins
It raises waste
optionExplanations: Right: volume charging rewards reduction.
Wrong: it's a charge, not free.
Wrong: it prices waste, not bans bins.
Wrong: it reduces waste.

standard.question: Why does 'pay-as-you-throw' cut household waste, and what design risk must be managed?
options: Charging by volume incentivises reduction and sorting, but it must stay fair to poorer households
It is free, so it changes nothing
It works by banning the use of bins
It increases the amount households throw away
optionExplanations: Correct: volume-based charging rewards waste reduction, but equity must be designed in to avoid unfair burdens.
Wrong: it is a pricing mechanism, not free.
Wrong: it prices waste; it does not ban bins.
Wrong: it reduces, not increases, household waste.

hard.question: Pay-as-you-throw cuts waste but carries which design risk?
options: It can be regressive, burdening poorer households unless equity is built in
It is free and therefore inert
It necessitates banning bins
It structurally increases waste
optionExplanations: Correct: fairness must be designed in to avoid disproportionate burdens.
Wrong: it is a charge, not free.
Wrong: it prices waste, not bans bins.
Wrong: it reduces waste.

roundEvents.title: The Final Audit
roundEvents.brief: As your term ends, an independent review weighs up what kind of city you've built — one that treats waste as a resource and thrives, or one still stuck in the throwaway mindset. The verdict will define your legacy.
roundEvents.caseFact: Optimistic "model" cities adopt circular metabolism; pessimistic ones stay linear and unsustainable.

actions.label: Publish the results and commit to keep improving
actions.resultExplain: Cements a circular path and accountability Invites scrutiny
actions.plainLabel: Publish the results and commit to keep improving
actions.plainMeaning: Be transparent and lock in continuous improvement.
pros: Cements a circular path and accountability
pays off most for circular cities.
actions.conceptLink: linear vs circular city as the city's lasting identity.

actions.label: Hold a quiet internal review
actions.resultExplain: Low effort. Little momentum or learning.
actions.plainMeaning: Keep the review low-key.
actions.conceptLink: linear vs circular city as the city's lasting identity.

actions.label: Declare success and move on
actions.resultExplain: Avoids scrutiny. No learning
actions.plainMeaning: Skip honest reflection.
actions.conceptLink: linear vs circular city as the city's lasting identity.

justify.question: What ultimately distinguishes a circular 'model' city from a linear one?
options: It reduces inputs and cycles outputs back into the system rather than discarding waste
It simply has a larger population
It has a bigger municipal budget alone
It operates a greater number of landfills
optionExplanations: Correct: the defining feature is closing resource loops, not size or spending.
Wrong: population does not determine metabolism type.
Wrong: budget alone does not make a city circular.
Wrong: more landfills indicate a linear, not circular, system.

roundEvents.scene: As your term ends, an independent review weighs up what kind of city you've built — one that treats waste as a resource and thrives, or one still stuck in the throwaway mindset. The verdict will define your legacy.
roundEvents.conceptLink: linear vs circular city as the city's lasting identity.

easy.question: What makes a city 'circular' not 'linear'?
options: It reuses outputs instead of discarding them
Bigger population
Bigger budget
More landfills
optionExplanations: Right: closing loops defines circularity.
Wrong: size isn't it.
Wrong: budget alone isn't it.
Wrong: more landfill is linear.

standard.question: What ultimately distinguishes a circular 'model' city from a linear one?
options: It reduces inputs and cycles outputs back into the system rather than discarding waste
It simply has a larger population
It has a bigger municipal budget alone
It operates a greater number of landfills
optionExplanations: Correct: the defining feature is closing resource loops, not size or spending.
Wrong: population does not determine metabolism type.
Wrong: budget alone does not make a city circular.
Wrong: more landfills indicate a linear, not circular, system.

hard.question: The ultimate marker of a circular city is...
options: minimising inputs and cycling outputs back into the system
a large population or budget
the number of landfills operated
high per-capita consumption
optionExplanations: Correct: closing resource loops defines circularity.
Wrong: scale is not the marker.
Wrong: more landfill signals linearity.
Wrong: high consumption is linear.

1.title: The Convenience Trap
1.brief: Walk down any high street and everything is wrapped in plastic — fruit in trays, drinks in double layers, snacks in pouches inside boxes. Shops love it because shiny packaging sells, but your bins are overflowing overnight. Residents are starting to notice the mountains of wrapping on collection day.
1.caseFact: Refrigeration and global transport made convenience packaging routine — packaging is now a marketing tool, not just protection.

actions.label: Charge a small fee on excess packaging
actions.resultExplain: Cuts rubbish at the source Shops and shoppers grumble about higher prices
actions.plainLabel: Charge a small fee on excess packaging
actions.plainMeaning: Make shops pay a little for wasteful wrapping, nudging them to use less.
cons: Shops and shoppers grumble about higher prices
the benefit takes time to show.
actions.conceptLink: where waste comes from — affluence and convenience packaging.

actions.label: Run a 'bring your own bag/cup' campaign
actions.resultExplain: Cheap and popular Only some people join in
actions.plainLabel: Run a 'bring your own bag/cup' campaign
actions.plainMeaning: Encourage people to refuse single-use packaging voluntarily.
cons: the effect is small and slow.
actions.conceptLink: where waste comes from — affluence and convenience packaging.

actions.label: Do nothing, just collect more
actions.resultExplain: No backlash Rubbish keeps climbing
actions.plainMeaning: Leave shops alone and keep picking up the extra rubbish.
pros: keeps shops and shoppers happy today.
cons: you've locked in a throwaway culture.
actions.conceptLink: where waste comes from — affluence and convenience packaging.

justify.question: Reduction sits above recycling in the waste hierarchy. Which reasoning BEST justifies that ranking?
options: Recycling is technically impossible for most packaging
Preventing waste avoids the energy, emissions and cost of later collecting, sorting and reprocessing it
Reduction generates more recyclable material to sell
Recycling inevitably contaminates groundwater
optionExplanations: Wrong: most packaging is recyclable; technical impossibility is not the basis of the hierarchy.
Correct: waste never created needs no downstream handling, so prevention avoids all those costs and impacts.
Wrong: reduction lowers the amount of material entering the stream — the opposite of generating more.
Wrong: leachate is a landfill issue, not an inherent feature of recycling.

1.scene: Walk down any high street and everything is wrapped in plastic — fruit in trays, drinks in double layers, snacks in pouches inside boxes. Shops love it because shiny packaging sells, but your bins are overflowing overnight. Residents are starting to notice the mountains of wrapping on collection day.
1.conceptLink: where waste comes from — affluence and convenience packaging.

easy.question: Why is using less packaging better than recycling it?
options: Less waste is created in the first place
It makes more rubbish
It is completely free
Recycling is banned
optionExplanations: Right: prevented waste never needs handling.
Wrong: reducing means less waste, not more.
Wrong: it has some cost, but avoids waste.
Wrong: recycling isn't banned.

options: Recycling is technically impossible for most packaging
Preventing waste avoids the energy, emissions and cost of later collecting, sorting and reprocessing it
Reduction generates more recyclable material to sell
Recycling inevitably contaminates groundwater
optionExplanations: Wrong: most packaging is recyclable; technical impossibility is not the basis of the hierarchy.
Correct: waste never created needs no downstream handling, so prevention avoids all those costs and impacts.

hard.question: Critics call a packaging tax 'slow and ineffective'. Which response best defends it on sustainability grounds?
options: By internalising disposal costs it shifts producer and consumer behaviour toward less packaging, even if benefits lag
It immediately eliminates all packaging waste
It guarantees higher recycling revenue this year
It has no effect on behaviour
optionExplanations: Correct: pricing externalities changes behaviour over time — the lag is expected, not a refutation.
Wrong: overstated; change is gradual.
Wrong: it targets reduction, not short-term revenue.
Wrong: that contradicts the rationale for the tax.

1.title: Built to be Binned
1.brief: Cheap gadgets and clothes are everywhere, and they're designed to be tossed, not fixed — when a phone battery dies, it's cheaper to buy a new phone than repair it. Landfills are filling with stuff that barely got used. People shrug; that's just how things are now.
1.caseFact: Many industries rely on built-in obsolescence — replacement is cheaper than repair, driving constant waste.

actions.label: Fund repair cafés and reuse shops
actions.resultExplain: Extends the life of products Costs money up front
actions.plainLabel: Fund repair cafés and reuse shops
actions.plainMeaning: Set up places where people can fix and re-sell things instead of binning them.
cons: only works if people choose to use them.
actions.conceptLink: "built-in obsolescence" — products made to be replaced, driving endless waste.

actions.label: Pass a 'right to repair' rule
actions.resultExplain: Tackles the root cause Manufacturers lobby hard against it
actions.plainMeaning: Force makers to design products that can actually be fixed.
actions.conceptLink: "built-in obsolescence" — products made to be replaced, driving endless waste.

actions.label: Just collect the extra rubbish
actions.resultExplain: Keeps streets tidy now Treats the symptom, not the cause
actions.plainMeaning: Expand collection to cope with all the discarded goods.
cons: Treats the symptom, not the cause
actions.conceptLink: "built-in obsolescence" — products made to be replaced, driving endless waste.

justify.question: Why does a 'right to repair' law tackle the waste problem more fundamentally than simply expanding collection?
options: It extends product lifespans, reducing the volume of goods entering the waste stream
It increases manufacturing output to meet demand
It removes the need for any recycling system
It lowers collection costs without changing consumption
optionExplanations: Correct: durable, repairable goods are replaced less often, cutting waste at its structural source.
Wrong: it slows replacement demand, so it tends to reduce, not increase, output.
Wrong: it reduces but does not eliminate end-of-life waste; recycling is still needed.
Wrong: that describes a collection efficiency, not addressing the root driver (built-in obsolescence).

1.scene: Cheap gadgets and clothes are everywhere, and they're designed to be tossed, not fixed — when a phone battery dies, it's cheaper to buy a new phone than repair it. Landfills are filling with stuff that barely got used. People shrug; that's just how things are now.
1.conceptLink: "built-in obsolescence" — products made to be replaced, driving endless waste.

easy.question: What do cheap goods 'made to be thrown away' do to a city's rubbish?
options: Increase it
Decrease it
Have no effect
Recycle it automatically
optionExplanations: Right: constant replacement piles up waste.
Wrong: they add waste, not reduce it.
Wrong: they clearly add to the bins.
Wrong: throwaway goods aren't auto-recycled.

standard.question: Why does a 'right to repair' law tackle the waste problem more fundamentally than simply expanding collection?
options: It extends product lifespans, reducing the volume of goods entering the waste stream
It increases manufacturing output to meet demand
It removes the need for any recycling system
It lowers collection costs without changing consumption
optionExplanations: Correct: durable, repairable goods are replaced less often, cutting waste at its structural source.
Wrong: it slows replacement demand, so it tends to reduce, not increase, output.
Wrong: it reduces but does not eliminate end-of-life waste; recycling is still needed.
Wrong: that describes a collection efficiency, not addressing the root driver (built-in obsolescence).

hard.question: Why might 'right to repair' regulation cut waste more durably than subsidising repair cafés?
options: It changes product design upstream, addressing the structural cause rather than only the symptom
Repair cafés increase manufacturing output
Regulation removes the need for any recycling
Subsidies always outperform regulation
optionExplanations: Correct: mandating repairable design tackles obsolescence at source, a more durable fix.
Wrong: cafés extend product life, not raise output.
Wrong: recycling is still needed.
Wrong: unsupported absolute claim.

1.title: Lockdown Leftovers
1.brief: Food delivery has exploded — every meal arrives in a stack of plastic tubs, bags and cutlery. In just two months the city has buried the equivalent of dozens of bus-loads of extra plastic. It's convenient, but the bins tell the story.
1.caseFact: Singapore households generated +1,334 tonnes of plastic during a two-month lockdown from deliveries.

actions.label: Launch a reusable container scheme
actions.resultExplain: Cuts packaging waste for good Needs a return-and-wash system to work
actions.plainMeaning: Set up returnable tubs so meals don't need throwaway packaging.
cons: Needs a return-and-wash system to work
actions.conceptLink: consumption habits spiking waste (the COVID delivery surge).

actions.label: Add recycling bins at food courts
actions.resultExplain: Captures materials if people sort right Lots of food-soiled plastic can't be recycled
actions.plainLabel: Add recycling bins at food courts
actions.plainMeaning: Put clearly-labelled bins where the takeaway waste is created.
pros: Captures materials if people sort right
cons: Lots of food-soiled plastic can't be recycled
actions.conceptLink: consumption habits spiking waste (the COVID delivery surge).

actions.label: Send it all to the tip
actions.resultExplain: No effort or cost now. Plastic lasts centuries
actions.plainLabel: Send it all to the tip
actions.plainMeaning: Just bury the extra packaging.
actions.conceptLink: consumption habits spiking waste (the COVID delivery surge).

justify.question: The delivery surge raised plastic waste sharply. Why is this difficult to solve through recycling alone?
options: Food-contaminated single-use plastics are often non-recyclable, keeping capture rates low
Most delivery packaging is biodegradable
Recycling mixed plastics is always profitable
Delivery packaging is predominantly glass
optionExplanations: Correct: contamination by food makes much of this plastic uneconomic or impossible to recycle, so it leaks to disposal.
Wrong: the packaging is largely non-biodegradable plastic.
Wrong: mixed-plastic recycling is frequently loss-making and price-volatile.
Wrong: it is overwhelmingly plastic, not glass.

1.scene: Food delivery has exploded — every meal arrives in a stack of plastic tubs, bags and cutlery. In just two months the city has buried the equivalent of dozens of bus-loads of extra plastic. It's convenient, but the bins tell the story.
1.conceptLink: consumption habits spiking waste (the COVID delivery surge).

easy.question: Why is takeaway packaging often hard to recycle?
options: It is dirty with food
It is made of gold
It is always spotless
It disappears by itself
optionExplanations: Right: food contamination makes much of it non-recyclable.
Wrong: it's cheap plastic, not gold.
Wrong: it's usually soiled.
Wrong: plastic does not vanish.

standard.question: The delivery surge raised plastic waste sharply. Why is this difficult to solve through recycling alone?
options: Food-contaminated single-use plastics are often non-recyclable, keeping capture rates low
Most delivery packaging is biodegradable
Recycling mixed plastics is always profitable
Delivery packaging is predominantly glass
optionExplanations: Correct: contamination by food makes much of this plastic uneconomic or impossible to recycle, so it leaks to disposal.
Wrong: the packaging is largely non-biodegradable plastic.
Wrong: mixed-plastic recycling is frequently loss-making and price-volatile.
Wrong: it is overwhelmingly plastic, not glass.

hard.question: A city adds recycling points for takeaway waste but capture stays low. What is the most likely explanation?
options: Food contamination makes much single-use plastic unrecyclable regardless of bin provision
Residents recycle too enthusiastically
Plastic is biodegradable, so it isn't collected
Recycling points reduce the waste generated
optionExplanations: Correct: contamination, not bin availability, is the binding constraint here.
Wrong: nonsensical as a cause of low capture.
Wrong: the plastic is non-biodegradable.
Wrong: conflates collection points with source reduction.

1.title: Edge City
1.brief: A brand-new neighbourhood is rising at the city's edge — homes are filling up before the bin lorries even have a route out there. Residents are already leaving bags on the kerb with nowhere for them to go. You can shape how this suburb deals with waste from day one.
1.caseFact: Urban sprawl pushes infrastructure outward; fringe areas often lack basic services first.

actions.label: Build full sorting + collection from day one
actions.resultExplain: Designs good habits in from the start Most expensive option up front.
actions.plainLabel: Build full sorting + collection from day one
actions.plainMeaning: Give the new suburb proper recycling and collection immediately.
pros: Designs good habits in from the start
actions.conceptLink: urban sprawl and getting services to the fringe.

actions.label: Basic bin collection only
actions.resultExplain: Cheaper Locks in throwaway habits
actions.plainMeaning: Just send lorries to pick up mixed rubbish.
actions.conceptLink: urban sprawl and getting services to the fringe.

actions.label: Let residents sort it out themselves
actions.resultExplain: Saves money today. Illegal dumping and resentment grow
actions.plainLabel: Let residents sort it out themselves
actions.plainMeaning: Leave the new suburb to manage its own waste for now.
actions.conceptLink: urban sprawl and getting services to the fringe.

justify.question: Why is building waste infrastructure into a new district from the outset more sustainable than retrofitting later?
options: It locks in collection-and-disposal patterns and infrastructure that are costly to reverse once established
New districts generate no waste in their first years
Retrofitting is always cheaper than building new
It removes the need for any collection service
optionExplanations: Correct: path dependency means early infrastructure and habits shape behaviour for decades; changing them later is expensive.
Wrong: occupied homes generate waste immediately.
Wrong: retrofitting established systems is generally more expensive and disruptive.
Wrong: collection is still required regardless of design.

1.scene: A brand-new neighbourhood is rising at the city's edge — homes are filling up before the bin lorries even have a route out there. Residents are already leaving bags on the kerb with nowhere for them to go. You can shape how this suburb deals with waste from day one.
1.conceptLink: urban sprawl and getting services to the fringe.

easy.question: Why set up recycling in a new neighbourhood from the start?
options: Habits are hard to change later
New areas make no waste
Fixing later is always cheaper
It removes the need for bins
optionExplanations: Right: early habits and infrastructure stick.
Wrong: occupied homes make waste at once.
Wrong: retrofitting is usually pricier.
Wrong: collection is still needed.

standard.question: Why is building waste infrastructure into a new district from the outset more sustainable than retrofitting later?
options: It locks in collection-and-disposal patterns and infrastructure that are costly to reverse once established
New districts generate no waste in their first years
Retrofitting is always cheaper than building new
It removes the need for any collection service
optionExplanations: Correct: path dependency means early infrastructure and habits shape behaviour for decades; changing them later is expensive.
Wrong: occupied homes generate waste immediately.
Wrong: retrofitting established systems is generally more expensive and disruptive.
Wrong: collection is still required regardless of design.

hard.question: Designing circular systems into a new district is defended on 'path dependency'. What does that mean?
options: Early infrastructure and habits become self-reinforcing, making later change costly
New districts are legally exempt from waste rules
Waste systems have no influence on behaviour
Costs always fall over time regardless of design
optionExplanations: Correct: initial choices lock in trajectories that are expensive to reverse.
Wrong: no such exemption exists.
Wrong: infrastructure shapes behaviour.
Wrong: retrofitting is typically costlier.

1.title: Streets of Refuse
1.brief: In the older, crowded districts the bin lorries simply can't keep up. Rubbish piles on corners, sometimes catching fire in the heat, and rats and flies are moving in. Families living there are getting sick, and they're angry.
1.caseFact: The 2012 World Bank report found 30–60% of urban waste in lower-income countries is uncollected.

actions.label: Invest in more lorries and routes
actions.resultExplain: Quickly removes the health hazard Costs money
actions.plainLabel: Invest in more lorries and routes
actions.plainMeaning: Expand the collection fleet to clear the backlog.
cons: you still have to dispose of all that waste somewhere.
actions.conceptLink: uncollected waste in fast-growing cities and its health toll.

actions.label: Hire local informal collectors
actions.resultExplain: Cheap and fast Needs organising and support to be safe and fair.
actions.plainMeaning: Pay the people already collecting waste by cart to do it officially.
pros: boosts recycling and gives people fair work
cons: Needs organising and support to be safe and fair.
actions.conceptLink: uncollected waste in fast-growing cities and its health toll.

actions.label: Leave it for now
actions.resultExplain: Saves money this year. Disease spreads
actions.plainMeaning: Don't act; focus the budget elsewhere.
actions.conceptLink: uncollected waste in fast-growing cities and its health toll.

justify.question: Uncollected waste in dense low-income districts is primarily a sustainability concern because it...
options: creates disease vectors and contaminates water, undermining the social and environmental pillars of SUD
directly lowers national GDP figures
is purely an aesthetic nuisance
raises recycling rates through informal picking
optionExplanations: Correct: accumulating waste breeds pests and pollutes water, harming health (social) and ecosystems (environmental).
Wrong: any GDP effect is indirect; the core concern is health and environmental harm.
Wrong: it is a serious health hazard, not merely unsightly.
Wrong: informal picking may occur, but it does not offset the public-health emergency and is not the primary concern.

1.scene: In the older, crowded districts the bin lorries simply can't keep up. Rubbish piles on corners, sometimes catching fire in the heat, and rats and flies are moving in. Families living there are getting sick, and they're angry.
1.conceptLink: uncollected waste in fast-growing cities and its health toll.

easy.question: Why is rubbish left uncollected in the streets dangerous?
options: It spreads disease and pests
It only looks untidy
It keeps people healthy
It has no effect
optionExplanations: Right: piles breed pests and pollute water.
Wrong: it's a health hazard, not just messy.
Wrong: it harms, not helps, health.
Wrong: it has serious effects.

standard.question: Uncollected waste in dense low-income districts is primarily a sustainability concern because it...
options: creates disease vectors and contaminates water, undermining the social and environmental pillars of SUD
directly lowers national GDP figures
is purely an aesthetic nuisance
raises recycling rates through informal picking
optionExplanations: Correct: accumulating waste breeds pests and pollutes water, harming health (social) and ecosystems (environmental).
Wrong: any GDP effect is indirect; the core concern is health and environmental harm.
Wrong: it is a serious health hazard, not merely unsightly.
Wrong: informal picking may occur, but it does not offset the public-health emergency and is not the primary concern.

hard.question: In low-income cities, which mechanism most directly turns uncollected waste into a public-health crisis?
options: Disease vectors breeding in waste while leachate contaminates water supplies
The aesthetic decline of neighbourhoods
Reduced property-tax revenue
Increased informal recycling activity
optionExplanations: Correct: vectors and water contamination are the direct disease pathways.
Wrong: appearance is secondary to health.
Wrong: a fiscal effect, not the health mechanism.
Wrong: a side-effect, not the cause of the crisis.

1.title: Sort It Out
1.brief: Your team proposes a city-wide scheme: give every household separate bins for paper, glass, plastic and food scraps, and make sorting the easy, normal thing to do. It could transform how the city handles waste — if people get on board.
1.caseFact: Copenhagen made source separation an easy default at household level for paper, glass, metal, plastic, organics.

actions.label: Full kerbside sorting for every home
actions.resultExplain: Big jump in recycling Expensive to launch
actions.plainLabel: Full kerbside sorting for every home
actions.plainMeaning: Roll out separate bins citywide and collect each stream.
cons: flops if households don't sort properly.
actions.conceptLink: household recycling only works with real participation.

actions.label: Just set up drop-off points
actions.resultExplain: Much cheaper Lower take-up
actions.plainMeaning: Place recycling banks around the city for people to use.
actions.conceptLink: household recycling only works with real participation.

actions.label: Wait until the budget is healthier
actions.resultExplain: Saves money today. You lose early momentum and stay a throwaway city.
actions.plainLabel: Wait until the budget is healthier
actions.plainMeaning: Delay recycling for now.
cons: You lose early momentum and stay a throwaway city.
actions.conceptLink: household recycling only works with real participation.

justify.question: Heavily-funded source-separation schemes can still fail. Which factor is the decisive variable?
options: Citizen participation and low contamination, since mixed/contaminated streams cannot be economically reprocessed
The colour scheme of the bins
The size of the municipal budget on its own
Proximity of homes to the nearest landfill
optionExplanations: Correct: recycling economics depend on clean, well-sorted material; without participation, contamination collapses the system.
Wrong: bin design is marginal compared with sorting behaviour.
Wrong: money buys infrastructure but cannot force correct sorting — participation is the binding constraint.
Wrong: distance to landfill does not determine recycling success.

1.scene: Your team proposes a city-wide scheme: give every household separate bins for paper, glass, plastic and food scraps, and make sorting the easy, normal thing to do. It could transform how the city handles waste — if people get on board.
1.conceptLink: household recycling only works with real participation.

easy.question: Recycling bins only work well if...
options: people actually sort their waste
the bins are a nice colour
the city is rich
a landfill is nearby
optionExplanations: Right: sorting by residents is essential.
Wrong: colour barely matters.
Wrong: money alone can't force sorting.
Wrong: landfill distance is irrelevant.

standard.question: Heavily-funded source-separation schemes can still fail. Which factor is the decisive variable?
options: Citizen participation and low contamination, since mixed/contaminated streams cannot be economically reprocessed
The colour scheme of the bins
The size of the municipal budget on its own
Proximity of homes to the nearest landfill
optionExplanations: Correct: recycling economics depend on clean, well-sorted material; without participation, contamination collapses the system.
Wrong: bin design is marginal compared with sorting behaviour.
Wrong: money buys infrastructure but cannot force correct sorting — participation is the binding constraint.
Wrong: distance to landfill does not determine recycling success.

hard.question: Two cities invest equally in source separation; one recycles far more. The decisive difference is most likely...
options: participation and contamination levels, which determine how much material is truly recoverable
the brand of collection vehicles
proximity to the nearest incinerator
the size of the city's debt
optionExplanations: Correct: behaviour and material quality drive recovery once investment is equal.
Wrong: vehicle brand is immaterial.
Wrong: incinerator distance is irrelevant.
Wrong: debt does not determine recycling capture.

1.title: Factory Lines
1.brief: A new industrial park has opened, bringing jobs and tax money — and a flood of commercial and factory waste that's very different from household rubbish. Some of it is bulky, some hazardous, all of it lands on your disposal system.
1.caseFact: Urban waste is not just domestic; it comes from industrial and commercial activity too.

actions.label: Require factories to audit and reuse waste
actions.resultExplain: Pushes industry to be circular Some firms complain about red tape.
actions.plainLabel: Require factories to audit and reuse waste
actions.plainMeaning: Make businesses track their waste and reuse what they can.
cons: Some firms complain about red tape.
actions.conceptLink: city waste is industrial and commercial, not just domestic.

actions.label: Charge factories a disposal fee
actions.resultExplain: Raises useful revenue Some firms may cut corners or dump illegally.
actions.plainMeaning: Make businesses pay for what they throw away.
pros: gives a mild nudge to waste less.
cons: Some firms may cut corners or dump illegally.
actions.conceptLink: city waste is industrial and commercial, not just domestic.

actions.label: Take their waste into the general system
actions.resultExplain: Keeps businesses happy and attracts more. Overloads your disposal
actions.plainLabel: Take their waste into the general system
actions.plainMeaning: Handle factory waste alongside household rubbish.
pros: Keeps businesses happy and attracts more.
actions.conceptLink: city waste is industrial and commercial, not just domestic.

justify.question: Planning a city's waste system only around household rubbish is inadequate because...
options: industrial and commercial activity generate large, varied and sometimes hazardous streams the system must handle
households are the sole source of hazardous waste
industry produces no waste of its own
commercial waste is automatically recycled
optionExplanations: Correct: urban waste includes substantial industrial/commercial flows with different handling needs.
Wrong: industry, not households, is the main source of many hazardous wastes.
Wrong: industrial processes are significant waste generators.
Wrong: commercial waste is not recycled by default; it must be managed.

1.scene: A new industrial park has opened, bringing jobs and tax money — and a flood of commercial and factory waste that's very different from household rubbish. Some of it is bulky, some hazardous, all of it lands on your disposal system.
1.conceptLink: city waste is industrial and commercial, not just domestic.

easy.question: Besides homes, what else creates a city's waste?
options: Factories and businesses
Only households
Nothing else
Rivers
optionExplanations: Right: industry and commerce produce lots of waste.
Wrong: homes aren't the only source.
Wrong: many sources exist.
Wrong: rivers aren't a waste source.

standard.question: Planning a city's waste system only around household rubbish is inadequate because...
options: industrial and commercial activity generate large, varied and sometimes hazardous streams the system must handle
households are the sole source of hazardous waste
industry produces no waste of its own
commercial waste is automatically recycled
optionExplanations: Correct: urban waste includes substantial industrial/commercial flows with different handling needs.
Wrong: industry, not households, is the main source of many hazardous wastes.
Wrong: industrial processes are significant waste generators.
Wrong: commercial waste is not recycled by default; it must be managed.

hard.question: Why can attracting industry boost the economy yet worsen the sustainability scorecard at once?
options: It adds revenue but also generates varied and hazardous waste the system must absorb
Industry produces no waste, so there is no trade-off
Economic growth always improves environmental indicators
Hazardous waste is automatically recycled
optionExplanations: Correct: economic and environmental indicators can move in opposite directions — a real trade-off.
Wrong: industry is a major waste source.
Wrong: growth and environment can conflict.
Wrong: hazardous waste needs deliberate handling.

1.title: Wrap and Throw
1.brief: With no proper sewers or collection in the poorest districts, people wrap their waste in paper and throw it straight into the river — the "wrap and throw" habit. The water downstream is turning foul, and the wells nearby are no longer safe to drink from.
1.caseFact: In Manila, human waste is wrapped in newspaper and thrown into rivers — the "wrap and throw" method.

actions.label: Build basic sanitation and collection
actions.resultExplain: Fixes the real cause Expensive and takes time to build.
actions.plainMeaning: Give these districts toilets, sewers and rubbish pickup.
cons: Expensive and takes time to build.
actions.conceptLink: how dumping in water spreads pollution far downstream.

actions.label: Clean the river and run awareness drives
actions.resultExplain: Visible quick win The waste still needs somewhere proper to go.
actions.plainLabel: Clean the river and run awareness drives
actions.plainMeaning: Clear the waterway and teach people why dumping harms them.
cons: The waste still needs somewhere proper to go.
actions.conceptLink: how dumping in water spreads pollution far downstream.

actions.label: Tolerate it
actions.resultExplain: Costs nothing now. Wells and the sea get poisoned
actions.plainMeaning: Do nothing about the river dumping.
cons: Wells and the sea get poisoned
actions.conceptLink: how dumping in water spreads pollution far downstream.

justify.question: 'Wrap-and-throw' river dumping shows how local mismanagement becomes a regional problem because...
options: leachate and waste travel downstream, contaminating water sources and coasts far from the source
rivers neutralise all contaminants they carry
the impact is confined to the exact dumping point
it only affects local fish-market prices
optionExplanations: Correct: flowing water transports pollutants, spreading contamination across the catchment and to the sea.
Wrong: rivers transport, not neutralise, most contaminants.
Wrong: the whole point is that effects spread well beyond the dumping site.
Wrong: the harm is ecological and public-health, far broader than fish prices.

1.scene: With no proper sewers or collection in the poorest districts, people wrap their waste in paper and throw it straight into the river — the "wrap and throw" habit. The water downstream is turning foul, and the wells nearby are no longer safe to drink from.
1.conceptLink: how dumping in water spreads pollution far downstream.

easy.question: Why does throwing rubbish in a river harm faraway places?
options: It flows downstream and pollutes
It stays in one spot
Rivers clean all waste
It only affects fish prices
optionExplanations: Right: water carries pollution far away.
Wrong: it spreads, not stays.
Wrong: rivers carry, not clean, waste.
Wrong: harm is far broader than prices.

standard.question: 'Wrap-and-throw' river dumping shows how local mismanagement becomes a regional problem because...
options: leachate and waste travel downstream, contaminating water sources and coasts far from the source
rivers neutralise all contaminants they carry
the impact is confined to the exact dumping point
it only affects local fish-market prices
optionExplanations: Correct: flowing water transports pollutants, spreading contamination across the catchment and to the sea.
Wrong: rivers transport, not neutralise, most contaminants.
Wrong: the whole point is that effects spread well beyond the dumping site.
Wrong: the harm is ecological and public-health, far broader than fish prices.

hard.question: 'Wrap and throw' is best understood as a symptom of which underlying failure?
options: Absent sanitation and collection, leaving residents no managed alternative
Excessive recycling enthusiasm
Over-investment in landfills
A surplus of clean water
optionExplanations: Correct: it is a coping response to missing infrastructure.
Wrong: unrelated to recycling.
Wrong: the problem is too little infrastructure, not too much.
Wrong: water scarcity/quality is the consequence, not the cause.

1.title: Teach the Future
1.brief: Teachers want to bring waste and recycling into every classroom — turning kids into the city's keenest sorters and gentle nags at home. It won't show up in this year's numbers, but it could change the city's habits for a generation.
1.caseFact: Changing public attitudes — linking waste to climate and making sorting logical — underpins Copenhagen's success.

actions.label: City-wide schools programme
actions.resultExplain: Builds lasting recycling habits cheaply Slow to show in the metrics.
actions.plainMeaning: Teach every school about reducing and sorting waste.
cons: Slow to show in the metrics.
actions.conceptLink: attitudes and participation are what make recycling work.

actions.label: One-off advertising campaign
actions.resultExplain: Cheap Limited reach
actions.plainMeaning: Run a short media push on recycling.
actions.conceptLink: attitudes and participation are what make recycling work.

actions.label: Skip it
actions.resultExplain: No cost. Habits never shift
actions.plainMeaning: Don't invest in education.
actions.conceptLink: attitudes and participation are what make recycling work.

justify.question: Waste education yields little in the current year. On what grounds is the investment still defended?
options: Participation is the binding constraint on whether reduce/recycle systems actually work
Bins sort waste automatically regardless of behaviour
Education removes the need for any infrastructure
Public attitudes have no measurable effect on recycling outcomes
optionExplanations: Correct: even good infrastructure fails without behaviour change, so building participation is a long-term enabler.
Wrong: sorting depends on people, not the bins themselves.
Wrong: education complements but cannot replace collection and processing infrastructure.
Wrong: participation rates strongly determine real recycling performance.

1.scene: Teachers want to bring waste and recycling into every classroom — turning kids into the city's keenest sorters and gentle nags at home. It won't show up in this year's numbers, but it could change the city's habits for a generation.
1.conceptLink: attitudes and participation are what make recycling work.

easy.question: Why teach children about recycling?
options: It builds good habits for the future
Bins sort waste by themselves
It replaces all bins
Habits don't matter
optionExplanations: Right: participation depends on attitudes.
Wrong: bins don't sort themselves.
Wrong: education adds to, not replaces, infrastructure.
Wrong: habits strongly affect recycling.

standard.question: Waste education yields little in the current year. On what grounds is the investment still defended?
options: Participation is the binding constraint on whether reduce/recycle systems actually work
Bins sort waste automatically regardless of behaviour
Education removes the need for any infrastructure
Public attitudes have no measurable effect on recycling outcomes
optionExplanations: Correct: even good infrastructure fails without behaviour change, so building participation is a long-term enabler.
Wrong: sorting depends on people, not the bins themselves.
Wrong: education complements but cannot replace collection and processing infrastructure.
Wrong: participation rates strongly determine real recycling performance.

hard.question: Education spending yields no metric gain this year. On what basis is it still rational?
options: It builds future participation, the binding constraint on whether recovery systems succeed
It substitutes entirely for collection infrastructure
It produces immediate measurable recycling gains
Attitudes are irrelevant to system performance
optionExplanations: Correct: it is a long-term investment in the factor that limits recovery.
Wrong: it complements, not replaces, infrastructure.
Wrong: that contradicts the premise of a lag.
Wrong: attitudes strongly shape outcomes.

1.title: Digital Discards
1.brief: Old phones, laptops and chargers are piling up as people upgrade constantly. This "e-waste" is tricky — it's full of toxic metals, but also valuable materials worth recovering. Right now most of it is just getting tossed in with normal rubbish.
1.caseFact: Electronics rely on rare-earth and critical raw materials; e-waste contains toxic and valuable components.

actions.label: Set up e-waste take-back and recovery
actions.resultExplain: Recovers precious materials and income Costs to set up specialist handling.
actions.plainLabel: Set up e-waste take-back and recovery
actions.plainMeaning: Collect old electronics and recover the valuable metals inside.
pros: keeps toxins out of the ground.
cons: Costs to set up specialist handling.
actions.conceptLink: e-waste as both a hazard and a recoverable resource ("urban mining").

actions.label: Open a hazardous-waste drop-off
actions.resultExplain: Stops the worst contamination Recovers less of the value.
actions.plainMeaning: Give people a safe place to hand in dangerous items.
actions.conceptLink: e-waste as both a hazard and a recoverable resource ("urban mining").

actions.label: Mix it into normal rubbish
actions.resultExplain: Cheapest, no effort. Toxic metals leak into soil and water
actions.plainMeaning: Treat e-waste like any other waste.
cons: Toxic metals leak into soil and water
actions.conceptLink: e-waste as both a hazard and a recoverable resource ("urban mining").

justify.question: Why should e-waste be treated as a resource stream rather than ordinary rubbish?
options: It contains recoverable critical materials and hazardous substances that must be contained
It is biodegradable and harmless if buried
It cannot be recycled by any known method
It has no economic value
optionExplanations: Correct: e-waste holds valuable critical/rare-earth materials and toxics, so recovery captures value and prevents contamination.
Wrong: it is non-biodegradable and leaches heavy metals.
Wrong: established 'urban mining' processes recover its materials.
Wrong: the recoverable metals give it real economic value.

1.scene: Old phones, laptops and chargers are piling up as people upgrade constantly. This "e-waste" is tricky — it's full of toxic metals, but also valuable materials worth recovering. Right now most of it is just getting tossed in with normal rubbish.
1.conceptLink: e-waste as both a hazard and a recoverable resource ("urban mining").

easy.question: Why is old electronics ('e-waste') special?
options: It has valuable metals and toxic parts
It is food waste
It is harmless and rots away
It has no value
optionExplanations: Right: it holds recoverable metals and toxics.
Wrong: it isn't food waste.
Wrong: it's toxic and non-biodegradable.
Wrong: its metals have real value.

standard.question: Why should e-waste be treated as a resource stream rather than ordinary rubbish?
options: It contains recoverable critical materials and hazardous substances that must be contained
It is biodegradable and harmless if buried
It cannot be recycled by any known method
It has no economic value
optionExplanations: Correct: e-waste holds valuable critical/rare-earth materials and toxics, so recovery captures value and prevents contamination.
Wrong: it is non-biodegradable and leaches heavy metals.
Wrong: established 'urban mining' processes recover its materials.
Wrong: the recoverable metals give it real economic value.

hard.question: Why is e-waste described as simultaneously a hazard and an opportunity?
options: It contains toxic substances needing containment and critical materials worth recovering
It is biodegradable yet valuable
It is harmless but worthless
It is both impossible to recycle and toxic
optionExplanations: Correct: the dual nature — risk and resource — defines how it should be handled.
Wrong: it is not biodegradable.
Wrong: it is hazardous and valuable, not harmless/worthless.
Wrong: it is recyclable via urban mining.

2.title: The Last Cell
2.brief: The city's main rubbish tip is nearly full — engineers reckon it has less than a year left. Finding new land near a crowded city is hard and unpopular, and the trucks keep rolling in every day. You need a plan before the gates are forced shut.
2.caseFact: Cities are running out of landfill space; finding suitable land near dense urban areas is increasingly hard.

actions.label: Build a proper sealed landfill
actions.resultExplain: Buys safe disposal space Expensive
actions.plainMeaning: Construct a modern, lined tip that won't leak into the environment.
pros: far cleaner than an open tip.
cons: the space is still finite and will fill again.
actions.conceptLink: cities are running out of landfill space.

actions.label: Open a cheap open dump
actions.resultExplain: Instant relief Poisons land, air and water
actions.plainMeaning: Just start tipping rubbish on open ground.
actions.conceptLink: cities are running out of landfill space.

actions.label: Cut the rubbish coming in with recycling
actions.resultExplain: Tackles the real problem Slower
actions.plainLabel: Cut the rubbish coming in with recycling
actions.plainMeaning: Reduce how much waste needs burying in the first place.
actions.conceptLink: cities are running out of landfill space.

justify.question: Why is responding to a full landfill by simply opening another one regarded as an unsustainable 'trap' rather than a solution?
options: Land is finite and every new site reproduces lasting leachate, methane and land-opportunity costs
Landfills can be operated indefinitely at no environmental cost
Suitable land near cities is effectively unlimited
Landfills recycle the waste they receive
optionExplanations: Correct: it postpones the problem while consuming scarce land and locking in long-term pollution liabilities.
Wrong: landfills carry ongoing leachate, gas and monitoring costs.
Wrong: peri-urban land is scarce and contested, which is the core constraint.
Wrong: landfills store waste; they do not recycle it.

2.scene: The city's main rubbish tip is nearly full — engineers reckon it has less than a year left. Finding new land near a crowded city is hard and unpopular, and the trucks keep rolling in every day. You need a plan before the gates are forced shut.
2.conceptLink: cities are running out of landfill space.

easy.question: Why can't a city just keep building new tips?
options: Land runs out and tips pollute
Land is unlimited
Tips are free forever
Tips recycle the waste
optionExplanations: Right: land is finite and tips cause pollution.
Wrong: urban land is scarce.
Wrong: tips have ongoing costs.
Wrong: tips store, not recycle.

standard.question: Why is responding to a full landfill by simply opening another one regarded as an unsustainable 'trap' rather than a solution?
options: Land is finite and every new site reproduces lasting leachate, methane and land-opportunity costs
Landfills can be operated indefinitely at no environmental cost
Suitable land near cities is effectively unlimited
Landfills recycle the waste they receive
optionExplanations: Correct: it postpones the problem while consuming scarce land and locking in long-term pollution liabilities.
Wrong: landfills carry ongoing leachate, gas and monitoring costs.
Wrong: peri-urban land is scarce and contested, which is the core constraint.
Wrong: landfills store waste; they do not recycle it.

hard.question: Why is 'build another landfill' described as deferring rather than solving the problem?
options: It consumes finite land and creates long-lived liabilities without reducing waste generation
Landfills permanently neutralise the waste they receive
Land near cities is effectively abundant
It eliminates the need for collection
optionExplanations: Correct: it postpones the issue while adding liabilities and using scarce land.
Wrong: landfills store, not neutralise.
Wrong: peri-urban land is scarce.
Wrong: collection is still required.

2.title: The Cheap Option
2.brief: Budget hawks on the council are pushing hard for the cheapest fix: just dump it on open land and move on. It would save a fortune this year. But you've seen what open dumps do to the people who live near them.
2.caseFact: Over 90% of waste in low-income countries is openly dumped or burned, with serious consequences.

actions.label: Refuse — pay for a proper sealed tip
actions.resultExplain: Protects residents Strains the budget now.
actions.plainLabel: Refuse — pay for a proper sealed tip
actions.plainMeaning: Spend more to protect health and the environment.
actions.conceptLink: open dumping vs engineered disposal in poorer cities.

actions.label: Approve the open dump
actions.resultExplain: Big budget saving Severe pollution and health damage
actions.plainMeaning: Take the cheapest route.
cons: a problem that grows every year.
actions.conceptLink: open dumping vs engineered disposal in poorer cities.

actions.label: Compromise: controlled tipping with soil cover
actions.resultExplain: Cheaper than full engineering Still leaks and pollutes more than a sealed site.
actions.plainLabel: Compromise: controlled tipping with soil cover
actions.plainMeaning: A halfway option — cover the rubbish daily but skip full engineering.
cons: Still leaks and pollutes more than a sealed site.
actions.conceptLink: open dumping vs engineered disposal in poorer cities.

justify.question: What fundamentally distinguishes an engineered landfill from an open dump?
options: Containment engineering — lining, leachate treatment and gas control that limit contamination
Only the official name authorities give the site
Open dumps always cover a larger area
Engineered landfills simply accept more waste per day
optionExplanations: Correct: the defining difference is the engineered barriers and treatment that contain pollution.
Wrong: it is an engineering distinction, not a naming one.
Wrong: size is not the distinguishing feature.
Wrong: throughput is not what makes a site 'engineered'.

2.scene: Budget hawks on the council are pushing hard for the cheapest fix: just dump it on open land and move on. It would save a fortune this year. But you've seen what open dumps do to the people who live near them.
2.conceptLink: open dumping vs engineered disposal in poorer cities.

easy.question: What makes a proper landfill safer than an open dump?
options: Lining and controls that stop pollution leaking
Only its name
It is bigger
It takes more waste daily
optionExplanations: Right: engineering contains the pollution.
Wrong: it's not just a name.
Wrong: size isn't the point.
Wrong: throughput isn't the point.

standard.question: What fundamentally distinguishes an engineered landfill from an open dump?
options: Containment engineering — lining, leachate treatment and gas control that limit contamination
Only the official name authorities give the site
Open dumps always cover a larger area
Engineered landfills simply accept more waste per day
