Wrong: the intent and effect is to cut waste.

hard.question: The Polluter-Pays principle works by...
options: internalising external disposal costs into prices to redirect behaviour
banning recycling outright
subsidising pollution
mandating higher waste output
optionExplanations: Correct: making polluters bear costs changes incentives.
Wrong: it promotes recycling.
Wrong: it prices, not subsidises, pollution.
Wrong: it aims to cut output.

4.title: The Climate Ledger
4.brief: Your city's emissions — from bin lorries, dumps and furnaces — are climbing on the national climate scorecard. The spotlight is turning toward you to clean up the waste sector.
4.caseFact: Waste-burning emissions (~1.6 Bt CO₂e in 2016) are projected to reach 2.6 Bt by 2050.

actions.label: Switch to clean vehicles and capture emissions
actions.resultExplain: Cuts emissions at the source Investment needed.
actions.plainLabel: Switch to clean vehicles and capture emissions
actions.plainMeaning: Decarbonise the waste fleet and trap emissions.
actions.conceptLink: the waste sector's greenhouse-gas emissions.

actions.label: Buy carbon offsets
actions.resultExplain: Neutral on paper. Doesn't change your own system.
actions.plainMeaning: Pay to balance your emissions elsewhere.
actions.conceptLink: the waste sector's greenhouse-gas emissions.

actions.label: Carry on as normal
actions.resultExplain: No cost. Emissions and climate exposure grow.
actions.plainMeaning: Take no action.
actions.conceptLink: the waste sector's greenhouse-gas emissions.

justify.question: Why should emissions accounting explicitly include the waste sector?
options: Collection, dumping and burning together emit significant methane and CO2
Only manufacturing industries emit greenhouse gases
Waste handling is an emission-free activity
Waste emissions affect only indoor air
optionExplanations: Correct: the sector's transport, decomposition and combustion are real, sizeable emission sources.
Wrong: many sectors emit, including waste.
Wrong: collection and disposal are emission-intensive.
Wrong: the emissions are atmospheric, not confined indoors.

4.scene: Your city's emissions — from bin lorries, dumps and furnaces — are climbing on the national climate scorecard. The spotlight is turning toward you to clean up the waste sector.

easy.question: Does the waste system produce greenhouse gases?
options: Yes — trucks, dumps and burning all emit
No, none at all
Only factories do
Only indoors
optionExplanations: Right: the whole system emits.
Wrong: it's a real emitter.
Wrong: many sources emit, including waste.
Wrong: emissions are outdoors/global.

standard.question: Why should emissions accounting explicitly include the waste sector?
options: Collection, dumping and burning together emit significant methane and CO2
Only manufacturing industries emit greenhouse gases
Waste handling is an emission-free activity
Waste emissions affect only indoor air
optionExplanations: Correct: the sector's transport, decomposition and combustion are real, sizeable emission sources.
Wrong: many sectors emit, including waste.
Wrong: collection and disposal are emission-intensive.
Wrong: the emissions are atmospheric, not confined indoors.

hard.question: Why is excluding the waste sector from carbon accounting a significant omission?
options: Collection, decomposition and combustion are material methane and CO2 sources
Only heavy industry emits greenhouse gases
Waste handling is carbon-neutral
Its emissions are confined indoors
optionExplanations: Correct: the sector is a real, sizeable emitter that accounting must capture.
Wrong: many sectors emit.
Wrong: it is emission-intensive.
Wrong: emissions are atmospheric/global.

4.title: Unwanted Guests
4.brief: The disturbed, polluted land ringing the city has become a foothold for aggressive invasive plants and pests that are crowding out native wildlife. The damaged edges of your city are spreading the problem.
4.caseFact: Urbanisation creates disturbed environments where invasive species thrive along roads and edges.

actions.label: Run a habitat restoration programme
actions.resultExplain: Restores biodiversity and resilience. Costs money.
actions.plainMeaning: Rebuild native ecosystems to push back the invaders.
actions.conceptLink: how sprawl and degradation let invasive species take over.

actions.label: Targeted control only
actions.resultExplain: Holds the line cheaply. Doesn't restore the habitat.
actions.plainMeaning: Tackle the worst invasive hotspots.
actions.conceptLink: how sprawl and degradation let invasive species take over.

actions.label: Ignore it
actions.resultExplain: No spend. Native wildlife keeps disappearing.
actions.plainMeaning: Leave nature to fend for itself.
actions.conceptLink: how sprawl and degradation let invasive species take over.

justify.question: How does urban sprawl facilitate the spread of invasive species?
options: Disturbed edge habitats give aggressive non-natives an advantage over native species
Sprawl physically blocks all species movement
Urban expansion has no measurable ecological effect
Native species always prefer roadside habitats
optionExplanations: Correct: fragmented, disturbed margins favour opportunistic invaders over specialised natives.
Wrong: sprawl creates corridors and disturbance, not barriers.
Wrong: expansion measurably disrupts ecosystems.
Wrong: it is invasives, not natives, that thrive in disturbed verges.

4.scene: The disturbed, polluted land ringing the city has become a foothold for aggressive invasive plants and pests that are crowding out native wildlife. The damaged edges of your city are spreading the problem.
4.conceptLink: how sprawl and degradation let invasive species take over.

easy.question: How does city sprawl help invasive species?
options: Disturbed edges let them outcompete natives
It blocks them
It has no effect
Natives prefer roads
optionExplanations: Right: disturbed margins favour invaders.
Wrong: sprawl aids, not blocks, them.
Wrong: it disrupts ecosystems.
Wrong: invaders, not natives, thrive there.

standard.question: How does urban sprawl facilitate the spread of invasive species?
options: Disturbed edge habitats give aggressive non-natives an advantage over native species
Sprawl physically blocks all species movement
Urban expansion has no measurable ecological effect
Native species always prefer roadside habitats
optionExplanations: Correct: fragmented, disturbed margins favour opportunistic invaders over specialised natives.
Wrong: sprawl creates corridors and disturbance, not barriers.
Wrong: expansion measurably disrupts ecosystems.
Wrong: it is invasives, not natives, that thrive in disturbed verges.

hard.question: The link between sprawl and invasive species is best explained by...
options: habitat disturbance at urban edges favouring opportunistic non-natives
sprawl forming barriers to species movement
an absence of any ecological effect
native preference for disturbed land
optionExplanations: Correct: disturbed margins advantage invaders over specialists.
Wrong: sprawl creates corridors, not barriers.
Wrong: it disrupts ecosystems.
Wrong: invaders, not natives, exploit verges.

4.title: Mend, Don't End
4.brief: A grassroots movement of menders, tinkerers and second-hand sellers is booming, and they want the city's backing to grow. They could help wean the city off its buy-new-throw-away habit.
4.caseFact: Reuse cuts the waste stream while letting consumers achieve high waste reduction.

actions.label: Fund a city-wide reuse economy
actions.resultExplain: Builds a lasting circular culture and local jobs Costs to support.
actions.plainMeaning: Back repair, resale and remanufacturing across the city.
pros: Builds a lasting circular culture and local jobs
actions.conceptLink: reuse and the circular economy.

actions.label: Support a few flagship repair cafés
actions.resultExplain: Cheap Limited scale.
actions.plainLabel: Support a few flagship repair cafés
actions.plainMeaning: Help a handful of showcase projects.
actions.conceptLink: reuse and the circular economy.

actions.label: Leave it to the market
actions.resultExplain: No spend. The throwaway culture persists.
actions.plainMeaning: Don't get involved.
actions.conceptLink: reuse and the circular economy.

justify.question: Where does reuse sit in the waste hierarchy, and why?
options: Above recycling and below reduce, because it keeps products in use without the energy of reprocessing
At the very bottom, below landfill
It is not part of the waste hierarchy at all
Above reduction, as the single most preferred option
optionExplanations: Correct: reuse avoids reprocessing energy, so it ranks above recycling but below preventing waste entirely.
Wrong: landfill is the least preferred; reuse is near the top.
Wrong: reuse is an explicit tier of the hierarchy.
Wrong: reduction, not reuse, is the most preferred option.

4.scene: A grassroots movement of menders, tinkerers and second-hand sellers is booming, and they want the city's backing to grow. They could help wean the city off its buy-new-throw-away habit.

easy.question: Where does 'reuse' sit in the waste hierarchy?
options: Near the top, above recycling
At the very bottom
It's not on it
Above reducing
optionExplanations: Right: reuse ranks above recycling.
Wrong: landfill is the bottom.
Wrong: reuse is a key tier.
Wrong: reduce is the top, not reuse.

standard.question: Where does reuse sit in the waste hierarchy, and why?
options: Above recycling and below reduce, because it keeps products in use without the energy of reprocessing
At the very bottom, below landfill
It is not part of the waste hierarchy at all
Above reduction, as the single most preferred option
optionExplanations: Correct: reuse avoids reprocessing energy, so it ranks above recycling but below preventing waste entirely.
Wrong: landfill is the least preferred; reuse is near the top.
Wrong: reuse is an explicit tier of the hierarchy.
Wrong: reduction, not reuse, is the most preferred option.

hard.question: Reuse outranks recycling in the hierarchy because it...
options: retains product value without the energy and material losses of reprocessing
is merely cheaper than landfill
sits below disposal
is the single most preferred option overall
optionExplanations: Correct: avoiding reprocessing keeps more value and energy, placing reuse above recycling.
Wrong: cost is not the ranking basis.
Wrong: reuse is near the top, not below disposal.
Wrong: reduction is the most preferred, not reuse.

5.title: The Recyclers Nobody Sees
5.brief: Thousands of people in your city already make a living picking through rubbish, recovering and selling materials by hand. They recycle more than your official system does — but they work in filth, without protection, pay or recognition. Their future is in your hands.
5.caseFact: Cairo's ~30,000 zabbaleen recycle a huge share of waste; a pair with a cart serves 350 households a day.

actions.label: Bring them into the official system
actions.resultExplain: Cheap boost to recycling Needs organising and some funding.
actions.plainLabel: Bring them into the official system
actions.plainMeaning: Give informal pickers safe conditions, fair pay and a real role.
pros: gives people dignity, safety and income
actions.conceptLink: informal recyclers and the social side of sustainability.

actions.label: License them but don't support them
actions.resultExplain: Acknowledges their role cheaply. They still work in unsafe conditions.
actions.plainLabel: License them but don't support them
actions.plainMeaning: Recognise them without investing in their safety.
cons: They still work in unsafe conditions.
actions.conceptLink: informal recyclers and the social side of sustainability.

actions.label: Clear them out
actions.resultExplain: A "tidier", more formal-looking city. Destroys livelihoods and a big chunk of your recycling
actions.plainMeaning: Remove the informal pickers from the streets.
cons: Destroys livelihoods and a big chunk of your recycling
actions.conceptLink: informal recyclers and the social side of sustainability.

justify.question: Evaluating informal recyclers, why is integration generally preferable to eviction?
options: They already provide low-cost recovery; formalising improves safety, equity and recycling rates
They add waste to the system and therefore must be removed
Eviction reliably increases a city's recycling rate
They are easily and cheaply replaced by machinery
optionExplanations: Correct: they are existing, effective recovery infrastructure; integrating them improves outcomes on every pillar.
Wrong: they remove waste from the stream, not add to it.
Wrong: eviction destroys recovery capacity, lowering recycling.
Wrong: their dispersed manual sorting is hard and costly to replace.

5.scene: Thousands of people in your city already make a living picking through rubbish, recovering and selling materials by hand. They recycle more than your official system does — but they work in filth, without protection, pay or recognition. Their future is in your hands.
5.conceptLink: informal recyclers and the social side of sustainability.

easy.question: Why work with informal waste-pickers instead of removing them?
options: They already recycle cheaply; helping them is safer and greener
They add waste
Removing them boosts recycling
They're easy to replace
optionExplanations: Right: they're existing recyclers worth supporting.
Wrong: they remove waste.
Wrong: removal cuts recycling.
Wrong: they're hard to replace.

standard.question: Evaluating informal recyclers, why is integration generally preferable to eviction?
options: They already provide low-cost recovery; formalising improves safety, equity and recycling rates
They add waste to the system and therefore must be removed
Eviction reliably increases a city's recycling rate
They are easily and cheaply replaced by machinery
optionExplanations: Correct: they are existing, effective recovery infrastructure; integrating them improves outcomes on every pillar.
Wrong: they remove waste from the stream, not add to it.

Wrong: their dispersed manual sorting is hard and costly to replace.

hard.question: The strongest sustainability case for integrating informal recyclers is that it...
options: raises recovery while improving safety and equity, advancing all three SUD pillars
tidies the city's appearance only
reduces recycling to cut costs
replaces them with cheaper machines

Wrong: appearance is a weak, partial justification.
Wrong: integration raises recovery.
Wrong: their dispersed labour is hard to mechanise.

5.title: The Unserved
5.brief: In the informal settlements, no bin lorry has ever come. Rubbish piles between the homes, children play beside it, and disease is never far away. These residents are part of your city too — but they've been forgotten.
5.caseFact: Slums often lack any formal waste management, making them hard to render liveable.

actions.label: Set up community-run collection
actions.resultExplain: Affordable Needs setting up and ongoing support.
actions.plainMeaning: Help residents organise their own local waste service.
cons: Needs setting up and ongoing support.
actions.conceptLink: service gaps in slums and liveable cities for all (SDG 11).

actions.label: Place communal skips
actions.resultExplain: A cheap basic improvement. Limited reach
actions.plainMeaning: Drop large shared bins in the settlements.
actions.conceptLink: service gaps in slums and liveable cities for all (SDG 11).

actions.label: Leave it unserved
actions.resultExplain: Saves money. Entrenches inequality
actions.plainMeaning: Don't extend service there.
actions.conceptLink: service gaps in slums and liveable cities for all (SDG 11).

justify.question: How does extending waste services to informal settlements relate to SDG 11 (Sustainable Cities)?
options: Inclusive basic services for all residents are central to liveable, sustainable cities
SDG 11 concerns only energy supply
Only high-income districts count toward sustainability
Service provision has no link to sustainability goals
optionExplanations: Correct: leaving districts unserved undermines the inclusivity and liveability that SDG 11 targets.
Wrong: SDG 11 is about cities and communities, not solely energy.
Wrong: sustainability requires inclusion of all residents, not just the wealthy.
Wrong: equitable services are explicitly part of sustainable urban development.

5.scene: In the informal settlements, no bin lorry has ever come. Rubbish piles between the homes, children play beside it, and disease is never far away. These residents are part of your city too — but they've been forgotten.
5.conceptLink: service gaps in slums and liveable cities for all (SDG 11).

easy.question: Why give waste collection to poor areas too?
options: A liveable city serves all residents
Only rich areas matter
It has no link to sustainability
SDG 11 is about energy
optionExplanations: Right: inclusion is central to sustainable cities.
Wrong: all residents count.

options: sustainable cities require inclusive basic services for all residents
SDG 11 concerns only energy supply
only formal districts are counted

optionExplanations: Correct: inclusivity and liveability for all are central to SDG 11.

5.title: Vectors
5.brief: It's happened: rotting, uncollected rubbish has bred swarms of mosquitoes and rats, and now dengue and cholera are sweeping through a district. Hospitals are filling. People are demanding to know why their warnings were ignored.
5.caseFact: Uncollected waste breeds rats, mosquitoes and flies, spreading cholera, dengue and respiratory illness.

actions.label: Launch an emergency clean-up and pest control
actions.resultExplain: Stops the outbreak at its source Costly emergency spending.
actions.plainLabel: Launch an emergency clean-up and pest control
actions.plainMeaning: Clear the waste and wipe out the breeding grounds fast.
pros: Stops the outbreak at its source
actions.conceptLink: waste as a public-health emergency.

actions.label: Treat the sick only
actions.resultExplain: Helps patients now. The breeding grounds remain
actions.plainMeaning: Focus on medical care without clearing the cause.
actions.conceptLink: waste as a public-health emergency.

actions.label: Downplay it
actions.resultExplain: Cheapest. The epidemic and public anger spread.
actions.plainMeaning: Minimise the crisis publicly.
cons: The epidemic and public anger spread.
actions.conceptLink: waste as a public-health emergency.

justify.question: Why is poorly managed waste classified as a public-health issue, not only an environmental one?
options: It breeds disease vectors and contaminates water, causing illnesses such as cholera and dengue
It merely produces an unpleasant smell
Disease and waste are essentially unrelated
Exposure to waste improves community immunity
optionExplanations: Correct: accumulating waste directly drives vector-borne and water-borne disease in the population.
Wrong: odour is trivial beside the actual disease burden.
Wrong: there is a well-established causal link.
Wrong: exposure raises illness, not immunity.

5.scene: It's happened: rotting, uncollected rubbish has bred swarms of mosquitoes and rats, and now dengue and cholera are sweeping through a district. Hospitals are filling. People are demanding to know why their warnings were ignored.

easy.question: Why is uncollected waste a health problem?
options: It breeds pests and spreads disease
It only smells
Waste and disease are unrelated

optionExplanations: Right: it drives vector- and water-borne disease.
Wrong: odour is the least of it.
Wrong: there's a clear link.
Wrong: it harms health.

standard.question: Why is poorly managed waste classified as a public-health issue, not only an environmental one?
options: It breeds disease vectors and contaminates water, causing illnesses such as cholera and dengue
It merely produces an unpleasant smell
Disease and waste are essentially unrelated
Exposure to waste improves community immunity
optionExplanations: Correct: accumulating waste directly drives vector-borne and water-borne disease in the population.
Wrong: odour is trivial beside the actual disease burden.
Wrong: there is a well-established causal link.
Wrong: exposure raises illness, not immunity.

hard.question: Why is the health burden of poor waste management framed as an equity issue?
options: It falls disproportionately on the poorest, least-served communities
It affects all residents identically
It is purely environmental, not social
Exposure improves community immunity

5.title: Collapse at the Dump
5.brief: Disaster strikes: a towering rubbish mound collapses (or catches fire), and the people who live and work on it are caught in it. The tragedy makes headlines, and the world sees the human cost of your city's waste.
5.caseFact: Life expectancy near Deonar is ~39 vs the state's 73.5; a 2016 fire burned for 3 months.

actions.label: Close the dump, relocate and retrain the pickers
actions.resultExplain: Ends the danger Costly
actions.plainLabel: Close the dump, relocate and retrain the pickers
actions.plainMeaning: Shut the dangerous site and give the workers safe new livelihoods.
pros: offers dignity and a fresh start
cons: you must replace the lost disposal capacity.
actions.conceptLink: the deadly dangers of open dumps and waste-picking.

actions.label: Add safety measures, keep it open
actions.resultExplain: Reduces risk somewhat The fundamental hazard remains.
actions.plainLabel: Add safety measures, keep it open
actions.plainMeaning: Make the dump a bit safer but keep using it.
actions.conceptLink: the deadly dangers of open dumps and waste-picking.

actions.label: Carry on as before
actions.resultExplain: No cost. More deaths and pollution
actions.plainMeaning: Take no action.
actions.conceptLink: the deadly dangers of open dumps and waste-picking.

justify.question: Why are open dumps considered a social-justice problem as well as an environmental one?
options: The urban poor bear concentrated health and safety harms from sites the wider city depends on
Their harms fall equally on every resident
Only the environment, not people, is affected
Dumps are demonstrably safe places to live and work
optionExplanations: Correct: the costs are borne disproportionately by the most vulnerable, raising questions of equity.
Wrong: the burden is highly uneven, concentrated on the poor.
Wrong: the human toll (e.g. reduced life expectancy) is central.
Wrong: dumps are hazardous, with documented health impacts.

5.scene: Disaster strikes: a towering rubbish mound collapses (or catches fire), and the people who live and work on it are caught in it. The tragedy makes headlines, and the world sees the human cost of your city's waste.
5.conceptLink: the deadly dangers of open dumps and waste-picking.

easy.question: Why are open dumps a justice issue?
options: The poor suffer the worst harm

Only nature suffers
Dumps are safe
optionExplanations: Right: the vulnerable bear the worst impacts.
Wrong: the burden is uneven.
Wrong: people suffer too.

standard.question: Why are open dumps considered a social-justice problem as well as an environmental one?
options: The urban poor bear concentrated health and safety harms from sites the wider city depends on
Their harms fall equally on every resident
Only the environment, not people, is affected
Dumps are demonstrably safe places to live and work
optionExplanations: Correct: the costs are borne disproportionately by the most vulnerable, raising questions of equity.

Wrong: the human toll (e.g. reduced life expectancy) is central.

options: The city's disposal convenience is subsidised by the safety of its most vulnerable
Environmental harm with no social dimension
A purely economic inefficiency
The absence of any trade-off
optionExplanations: Correct: it exposes how the poor bear the hidden costs of cheap disposal.
Wrong: it is profoundly social.
Wrong: it is a human tragedy, not mere inefficiency.

5.title: Lost Schooldays
5.brief: Many of the waste-pickers are children, sorting rubbish for family income instead of sitting in classrooms. Every day on the dump is a day not in school — and a future quietly slipping away.
5.caseFact: Manual scavenging often pulls children from education, trapping families in poverty.

actions.label: Offer schooling plus family stipends
actions.resultExplain: Breaks the poverty cycle Ongoing cost.
actions.plainMeaning: Pay families so children can go to school instead of picking waste.
actions.conceptLink: how waste-picking traps families and steals education.

actions.label: Ban child picking and enforce it
actions.resultExplain: Right in principle. Removes family income without offering an alternative.
actions.plainLabel: Ban child picking and enforce it
actions.plainMeaning: Make it illegal for children to work the dumps.
cons: Removes family income without offering an alternative.
actions.conceptLink: how waste-picking traps families and steals education.

actions.label: Ignore it
actions.resultExplain: No cost. Poverty passes to the next generation.
actions.plainMeaning: Take no action.
cons: Poverty passes to the next generation.
actions.conceptLink: how waste-picking traps families and steals education.

justify.question: How does children's involvement in waste-picking affect long-term development?
options: It displaces schooling, helping to perpetuate poverty across generations
It improves educational attainment
It carries no measurable social cost
It is reliably well-paid, secure work
optionExplanations: Correct: lost education traps families in a cycle of poverty, a long-term development harm.
Wrong: it removes children from school, harming attainment.
Wrong: the intergenerational cost is significant.
Wrong: scavenging is precarious and poorly paid.

5.scene: Many of the waste-pickers are children, sorting rubbish for family income instead of sitting in classrooms. Every day on the dump is a day not in school — and a future quietly slipping away.

easy.question: Why is children picking waste a long-term problem?
options: They miss school, staying poor
It boosts schooling
It has no social cost

optionExplanations: Right: lost schooling traps families in poverty.

options: It displaces schooling, helping to perpetuate poverty across generations

improves educational outcomes

5.title: Strength in Numbers
5.brief: The recyclers you've supported want to band together into a cooperative — pooling their materials, getting fair prices, and supplying local workshops. With a little backing, they could become a real engine of the circular economy.
5.caseFact: Scavenged materials feed small industries (e.g., Dar es Salaam's charcoal stoves and buckets).

actions.label: Back the co-op and link it to industry
actions.resultExplain: Turns picking into a stable, fair circular business Some funding and coordination needed.
actions.plainLabel: Back the co-op and link it to industry
actions.plainMeaning: Help them organise and connect them to buyers and workshops.
pros: Turns picking into a stable, fair circular business
actions.conceptLink: cooperatives turning informal recovery into stable circular supply chains.

actions.label: Provide equipment only
actions.resultExplain: A useful, cheap boost. Misses the bigger opportunity.
actions.plainMeaning: Give them tools but no market links.
actions.conceptLink: cooperatives turning informal recovery into stable circular supply chains.

actions.label: Decline
actions.resultExplain: No spend. Misses a cheap circularity win.
actions.plainMeaning: Don't support the cooperative.
actions.conceptLink: cooperatives turning informal recovery into stable circular supply chains.

justify.question: Why does linking recycler cooperatives to local industry strengthen circularity?
options: Recovered materials become manufacturing inputs, forming closed local value chains
It wastes the recovered materials
Local industry is unable to use recovered materials
It increases the volume sent to landfill
optionExplanations: Correct: feeding recovered materials back into production is the essence of a circular economy.
Wrong: the point is to use, not waste, the materials.
Wrong: many small industries rely on recovered inputs.
Wrong: it diverts material from landfill, not toward it.

easy.question: Why link recycler co-ops to local industry?
options: Recovered materials become inputs for making things
It wastes materials

5.title: Trash for Tokens
5.brief: Inspired by a famous scheme abroad, you could let residents swap sorted recyclables for bus tickets or fresh food. It makes recycling rewarding — and it especially helps poorer families who need the tokens most.
5.caseFact: Curitiba's Green Exchange Programme rewards recycling, boosting participation in low-income areas.

actions.label: Launch a 'recycle for tokens' scheme
actions.resultExplain: Huge boost to participation Costs to run the rewards.
actions.plainMeaning: Let people trade sorted waste for bus tickets or food.
actions.conceptLink: incentive schemes (the Curitiba Green Exchange).

actions.label: Pay cash for recyclables
actions.resultExplain: Works for many people. Less community spirit than tokens.
actions.plainMeaning: Buy back sorted materials for money.
actions.conceptLink: incentive schemes (the Curitiba Green Exchange).

actions.label: Make sorting mandatory, no reward
actions.resultExplain: Cheap. Resented
actions.plainMeaning: Force people to sort without any incentive.
actions.conceptLink: incentive schemes (the Curitiba Green Exchange).

justify.question: Why is Curitiba's 'recyclables-for-tokens' scheme so effective, especially in low-income areas?
options: It rewards participation with valued benefits, aligning incentives so people choose to take part
It penalises households that recycle
It functions without any citizen participation
It is compulsory and offers no rewards
optionExplanations: Correct: tangible rewards make recycling worthwhile for residents, driving high participation where it is needed most.
Wrong: it rewards, not penalises, recycling.
Wrong: participation is exactly what it mobilises.
Wrong: it is incentive-based, not a reward-free mandate.

5.scene: Inspired by a famous scheme abroad, you could let residents swap sorted recyclables for bus tickets or fresh food. It makes recycling rewarding — and it especially helps poorer families who need the tokens most.

easy.question: Why does rewarding recycling with tokens work?
options: People take part when it benefits them
It punishes recyclers
It needs no participation
It has no rewards
optionExplanations: Right: rewards drive participation.
Wrong: it rewards, not punishes.
Wrong: participation is the point.
Wrong: it's reward-based.

standard.question: Why is Curitiba's 'recyclables-for-tokens' scheme so effective, especially in low-income areas?
options: It rewards participation with valued benefits, aligning incentives so people choose to take part
It penalises households that recycle
It functions without any citizen participation
It is compulsory and offers no rewards
optionExplanations: Correct: tangible rewards make recycling worthwhile for residents, driving high participation where it is needed most.
Wrong: it rewards, not penalises, recycling.
Wrong: participation is exactly what it mobilises.
Wrong: it is incentive-based, not a reward-free mandate.

hard.question: Why are incentive schemes like Curitiba's especially effective in low-income areas?
options: They align personal benefit with recycling, overcoming participation barriers where need is high
They penalise non-participation harshly
They require no behaviour change
They function without any rewards
optionExplanations: Correct: tangible rewards mobilise participation precisely where it is hardest.
Wrong: they reward, not punish.
Wrong: they drive behaviour change.
Wrong: rewards are the mechanism.

5.title: The Women at the Sorting Line
5.brief: Much of the hand-sorting is done by women and children at home, with bare hands and no masks, surrounded by hazardous waste. They keep the system running while quietly paying for it with their health.
5.caseFact: In Cairo, women and children sort waste at home, facing direct health risks.

actions.label: Provide protective gear, clinics and safe facilities
actions.resultExplain: Protects the most exposed people Ongoing cost.
actions.plainLabel: Provide protective gear, clinics and safe facilities
actions.plainMeaning: Give the workers safety equipment and healthcare.
actions.conceptLink: worker health and equity in the social dimension of sustainability.

actions.label: Offer health screening only
actions.resultExplain: Detects problems early. Doesn't prevent the harm.
actions.plainMeaning: Check workers' health without changing conditions.
actions.conceptLink: worker health and equity in the social dimension of sustainability.

actions.label: Do nothing
actions.resultExplain: No cost. Preventable illness continues.
actions.plainMeaning: Leave conditions as they are.
actions.conceptLink: worker health and equity in the social dimension of sustainability.

justify.question: Why does the social pillar of sustainability concern WHO handles waste, not only how much?
options: Worker health, safety and equity are integral to sustainable development
Only the total tonnage of waste matters
Waste workers face no real health risks
Improving worker safety lowers recycling rates
optionExplanations: Correct: sustainability includes the wellbeing and fair treatment of the people doing the work.
Wrong: tonnage ignores the human dimension the social pillar addresses.
Wrong: hand-sorting hazardous waste carries serious risks.
Wrong: safer conditions support, not undermine, recovery.

5.scene: Much of the hand-sorting is done by women and children at home, with bare hands and no masks, surrounded by hazardous waste. They keep the system running while quietly paying for it with their health.
5.conceptLink: worker health and equity in the social dimension of sustainability.

easy.question: Why care who handles waste, not just how much?
options: Worker health and fairness matter
Only total tonnage matters
Workers face no risk
Safety lowers recycling
optionExplanations: Right: the social pillar covers worker wellbeing.
Wrong: tonnage ignores people.
Wrong: hand-sorting is risky.
Wrong: safety supports recovery.

standard.question: Why does the social pillar of sustainability concern WHO handles waste, not only how much?

hard.question: Focusing on who handles waste reflects which dimension of sustainability?
options: The social pillar — worker health, safety and equity
The purely economic pillar
The purely environmental pillar
None; only tonnage matters
optionExplanations: Correct: worker wellbeing is the social dimension of SUD.
Wrong: it is social, not only economic.
Wrong: it is social, not only environmental.
Wrong: the human dimension matters.

5.title: Cleared Out
5.brief: Developers are lobbying hard to bulldoze a waste-pickers' settlement and put up new buildings. It would mean quick development cash — but it would scatter a community that quietly recycles much of your city.
5.caseFact: Authorities increasingly see scavengers as recyclers to incorporate, not remove.

actions.label: Refuse and protect them
actions.resultExplain: Keeps recycling and community intact Foregoes development cash
actions.plainMeaning: Block the eviction and formally integrate the community.
actions.conceptLink: displacement of the urban poor vs incorporating them.

actions.label: Relocate them with support
actions.resultExplain: A more humane move. Disrupts their established recycling networks.
actions.plainMeaning: Move the community but help them resettle.
actions.conceptLink: displacement of the urban poor vs incorporating them.

actions.label: Allow the eviction
actions.resultExplain: Quick development gain. Recycling and trust collapse
actions.plainMeaning: Let developers clear the site.
actions.conceptLink: displacement of the urban poor vs incorporating them.

justify.question: Why might evicting waste-pickers paradoxically raise a city's total waste costs?
options: The city loses free recovery labour and must collect and dispose of more itself
Eviction increases recovery efficiency
Pickers add to, rather than reduce, the waste stream
Removing pickers has no budgetary effect
optionExplanations: Correct: the recovery they provided must now be paid for through formal collection and disposal.
Wrong: removing recovery capacity lowers efficiency.
Wrong: pickers divert waste from disposal.
Wrong: it shifts costs onto the municipal budget.

5.scene: Developers are lobbying hard to bulldoze a waste-pickers' settlement and put up new buildings. It would mean quick development cash — but it would scatter a community that quietly recycles much of your city.
5.conceptLink: displacement of the urban poor vs incorporating them.

easy.question: Why can removing waste-pickers cost more?
options: The city must now collect what they recovered
It boosts efficiency
Pickers add waste
No budget effect
optionExplanations: Right: their free recovery must be replaced.
Wrong: it cuts recovery.
Wrong: they divert waste.
Wrong: costs shift to the city.

standard.question: Why might evicting waste-pickers paradoxically raise a city's total waste costs?
options: The city loses free recovery labour and must collect and dispose of more itself
Eviction increases recovery efficiency
Pickers add to, rather than reduce, the waste stream
Removing pickers has no budgetary effect
optionExplanations: Correct: the recovery they provided must now be paid for through formal collection and disposal.
Wrong: removing recovery capacity lowers efficiency.
Wrong: pickers divert waste from disposal.
Wrong: it shifts costs onto the municipal budget.

hard.question: Why can evicting waste-pickers raise net municipal costs?
options: The city absorbs the recovery and disposal they previously provided for free
Eviction raises recovery efficiency
Pickers add to the waste stream
There is no fiscal consequence
optionExplanations: Correct: lost free labour must be replaced by paid services.
Wrong: it lowers recovery.
Wrong: they divert waste.
Wrong: costs shift onto the city.

5.title: Back to the Soil
5.brief: Neighbourhoods want to turn their food scraps and garden clippings into compost for local gardens, instead of sending heavy, smelly organics to the tip where they rot and leak methane. It's simple, local and satisfying.
5.caseFact: Organic waste returned to soil is a "biological product" re-entering the organic cycle.

actions.label: Roll out community composting
actions.resultExplain: Diverts heavy waste Needs some setup and participation.
actions.plainMeaning: Help neighbourhoods compost their organic waste locally.
actions.conceptLink: composting organics closes the natural nutrient loop.

actions.label: Build a central composting facility
actions.resultExplain: Efficient at scale. Less community involvement
actions.plainMeaning: Compost the city's organics at one big plant.
actions.conceptLink: composting organics closes the natural nutrient loop.

actions.label: Keep organics in the tip
actions.resultExplain: No effort. It rots into methane and fills the tip.
actions.plainMeaning: Bury food and garden waste as usual.
cons: It rots into methane and fills the tip.
actions.conceptLink: composting organics closes the natural nutrient loop.

justify.question: Why is diverting organic waste from landfill to composting beneficial on two fronts?
options: It cuts landfill methane while returning nutrients to the soil as compost
Organic waste cannot be composted
Landfilling organics is better for the climate
Organic waste does not decompose
optionExplanations: Correct: it both avoids potent methane emissions and closes the nutrient loop.
Wrong: organics are the ideal compost feedstock.
Wrong: landfilling organics generates methane, worsening the climate impact.
Wrong: organics decompose readily — anaerobically in landfill, aerobically in compost.

5.scene: Neighbourhoods want to turn their food scraps and garden clippings into compost for local gardens, instead of sending heavy, smelly organics to the tip where they rot and leak methane. It's simple, local and satisfying.
5.conceptLink: composting organics closes the natural nutrient loop.

easy.question: Why compost food/garden waste instead of burying it?
options: It cuts methane and makes useful compost
It can't be composted
Burying is better
It won't rot
optionExplanations: Right: composting avoids methane and returns nutrients.
Wrong: organics compost well.
Wrong: burying makes methane.
Wrong: organics rot readily.

standard.question: Why is diverting organic waste from landfill to composting beneficial on two fronts?
options: It cuts landfill methane while returning nutrients to the soil as compost
Organic waste cannot be composted
Landfilling organics is better for the climate
Organic waste does not decompose
optionExplanations: Correct: it both avoids potent methane emissions and closes the nutrient loop.
Wrong: organics are the ideal compost feedstock.
Wrong: landfilling organics generates methane, worsening the climate impact.
Wrong: organics decompose readily — anaerobically in landfill, aerobically in compost.

hard.question: Composting organics delivers a 'double dividend'. What is it?
options: Avoided landfill methane plus nutrient return to soils
Lower recycling rates but cheaper disposal
More methane but better compost
Neither a climate nor a soil benefit
optionExplanations: Correct: it cuts emissions and closes the nutrient loop simultaneously.
Wrong: it does not lower recycling.
Wrong: it reduces methane.
Wrong: it benefits both climate and soil.

6.title: The Danish Model
6.brief: It's the final stretch, and you can pass a landmark law: make those who create waste pay for it, taxing dumping heavily and rewarding recycling. Done right, it could reshape the city's habits for good — but it's a political fight.
6.caseFact: Denmark taxes landfill (€62.56/t) far above incineration (€6.69/t); it is illegal to landfill what can be incinerated.

actions.label: Pass a full polluter-pays law
actions.resultExplain: Powerful, lasting push toward recycling and reduction Politically costly
actions.plainMeaning: Tax waste creation hard, with the highest charges on dumping.
pros: Powerful, lasting push toward recycling and reduction
actions.conceptLink: comprehensive "polluter pays" taxation (the Denmark model).

actions.label: Bring in a disposal tax only
actions.resultExplain: Nudges waste up the hierarchy without full reform. Smaller impact.
actions.plainLabel: Bring in a disposal tax only
actions.plainMeaning: Tax dumping and burning, more gently.
pros: Nudges waste up the hierarchy without full reform.
actions.conceptLink: comprehensive "polluter pays" taxation (the Denmark model).

actions.label: No new tax
actions.resultExplain: Avoids backlash. Misses the chance to lock in a circular city.
actions.plainMeaning: Leave waste untaxed.
cons: Misses the chance to lock in a circular city.
actions.conceptLink: comprehensive "polluter pays" taxation (the Denmark model).

justify.question: Denmark taxes landfill far more heavily than incineration or recycling. What is the policy logic?
options: Differential pricing steers waste up the hierarchy toward less harmful options
It exists simply to maximise total tax revenue
Landfill is the cleanest option and should be encouraged
Recycling is the most harmful option and so is penalised
optionExplanations: Correct: charging most for the worst option nudges behaviour toward higher tiers of the hierarchy.
Wrong: the aim is behavioural redirection, not revenue.
Wrong: landfill is the least preferred, not the cleanest.
Wrong: recycling is favoured, not penalised, under such schemes.

6.scene: It's the final stretch, and you can pass a landmark law: make those who create waste pay for it, taxing dumping heavily and rewarding recycling. Done right, it could reshape the city's habits for good — but it's a political fight.

easy.question: Why tax dumping more than recycling?
options: To push waste toward better options
To make the most money
Dumping is cleanest
Recycling is worst
optionExplanations: Right: pricing the worst option steers behaviour up the hierarchy.
Wrong: the aim is behaviour, not revenue.
Wrong: dumping is the worst option.
Wrong: recycling is favoured.

standard.question: Denmark taxes landfill far more heavily than incineration or recycling. What is the policy logic?
options: Differential pricing steers waste up the hierarchy toward less harmful options
It exists simply to maximise total tax revenue
Landfill is the cleanest option and should be encouraged
Recycling is the most harmful option and so is penalised
optionExplanations: Correct: charging most for the worst option nudges behaviour toward higher tiers of the hierarchy.
Wrong: the aim is behavioural redirection, not revenue.
Wrong: landfill is the least preferred, not the cleanest.
Wrong: recycling is favoured, not penalised, under such schemes.

hard.question: Differential waste taxation (high on landfill, low on recycling) is designed to...
options: price externalities so behaviour moves up the waste hierarchy
maximise municipal revenue regardless of behaviour
reward landfill as the cleanest option
penalise recycling as the most harmful
optionExplanations: Correct: pricing the worst options steers behaviour toward better ones.
Wrong: the aim is behavioural, not revenue.
Wrong: landfill is least preferred.
Wrong: recycling is favoured.

6.title: Aiming for Zero
6.brief: You can commit the city to a bold, binding goal: send almost nothing to landfill, like the world's leading "zero-waste" cities. It's a statement of ambition that will demand real investment to back it up.
6.caseFact: Copenhagen sends <2% to landfill, recycles ~45%, and targets 70% under "Circular Copenhagen."

actions.label: Commit to a binding zero-waste roadmap
actions.resultExplain: A clear circular trajectory Demands sustained investment and public buy-in.
actions.plainLabel: Commit to a binding zero-waste roadmap
actions.plainMeaning: Set a legal target to virtually eliminate landfilling.
pros: inspiring and credible if backed up.
cons: Demands sustained investment and public buy-in.
actions.conceptLink: zero-waste-city targets (Copenhagen, Yokohama).

actions.label: Set a voluntary aspiration
actions.resultExplain: Signals intent cheaply. Weak follow-through.
actions.plainMeaning: Announce a goal without binding teeth.
actions.conceptLink: zero-waste-city targets (Copenhagen, Yokohama).

actions.label: Reject it as unrealistic
actions.resultExplain: Pragmatic. Forgoes the circular prize.
actions.plainMeaning: Don't make the pledge.
actions.conceptLink: zero-waste-city targets (Copenhagen, Yokohama).

justify.question: What makes a 'Zero Waste City' target credible rather than mere branding?
options: Integrated reduce/reuse/recycle systems backed by binding policy and citizen participation
A memorable slogan and logo
A complete ban on all consumption
Building additional landfill capacity
optionExplanations: Correct: credibility rests on enforceable systems and behaviour, not aspiration alone.
Wrong: branding without delivery is exactly the failure mode.
Wrong: zero waste targets throughput, not consumption bans.
Wrong: more landfill is the opposite of a zero-waste trajectory.

6.scene: You can commit the city to a bold, binding goal: send almost nothing to landfill, like the world's leading "zero-waste" cities. It's a statement of ambition that will demand real investment to back it up.

easy.question: What makes a 'zero waste' goal real?
options: Real systems and rules to back it
A slogan alone
Banning all shopping
More landfills
optionExplanations: Right: enforceable systems make it credible.
Wrong: a slogan isn't enough.
Wrong: it targets throughput, not shopping.
Wrong: more landfill is the opposite.

standard.question: What makes a 'Zero Waste City' target credible rather than mere branding?
options: Integrated reduce/reuse/recycle systems backed by binding policy and citizen participation
A memorable slogan and logo
A complete ban on all consumption
Building additional landfill capacity
optionExplanations: Correct: credibility rests on enforceable systems and behaviour, not aspiration alone.

hard.question: What distinguishes a credible zero-waste commitment from greenwashing?
options: Binding policy, integrated systems and measurable participation behind the target
A compelling slogan and brand
A pledge to ban consumption
Expanded landfill capacity
optionExplanations: Correct: enforceable substance, not messaging, makes it credible.
Wrong: branding is the greenwashing risk.
Wrong: it targets throughput, not consumption bans.
Wrong: more landfill contradicts the goal.

6.title: Industrial Symbiosis
6.brief: Your factories could be linked so that one company's waste becomes another's raw material — a clever web where almost nothing is thrown away. It takes coordination, but it could make your industry world-leading in circularity.
6.caseFact: "Technical products" can circulate continuously as materials in the industrial cycle.

actions.label: Build an eco-industrial park
actions.resultExplain: Creates closed material loops and local value Needs coordination and investment.
actions.plainMeaning: Cluster industries so they swap and reuse each other's by-products.
pros: Creates closed material loops and local value
actions.conceptLink: industrial symbiosis and the technical materials cycle.

actions.label: Set up a by-product exchange platform
actions.resultExplain: Lower-cost matchmaking for waste-as-resource. Less impact than a full park.
actions.plainLabel: Set up a by-product exchange platform
actions.plainMeaning: A lighter system matching waste to firms that can use it.
cons: Less impact than a full park.
actions.conceptLink: industrial symbiosis and the technical materials cycle.

actions.label: Keep the status quo
actions.resultExplain: No spend. Industrial materials stay wasteful.
actions.plainMeaning: Leave industry as is.
actions.conceptLink: industrial symbiosis and the technical materials cycle.

justify.question: What does 'industrial symbiosis' mean within a circular economy?
options: One firm's waste or by-product becomes another firm's raw material
Firms competing to extract the most virgin resources
Burning all industrial waste for energy
Exporting industrial waste to other countries
optionExplanations: Correct: it closes loops between firms, turning outputs into inputs.
Wrong: that is linear competition for virgin material.
Wrong: combustion is recovery, not symbiosis.
Wrong: exporting waste is displacement, not symbiosis.

6.scene: Your factories could be linked so that one company's waste becomes another's raw material — a clever web where almost nothing is thrown away. It takes coordination, but it could make your industry world-leading in circularity.

easy.question: What is 'industrial symbiosis'?
options: One firm's waste is another's raw material
Firms competing for resources
Burning all waste
Exporting waste
optionExplanations: Right: outputs become inputs between firms.
Wrong: that's linear competition.
Wrong: burning is recovery, not symbiosis.
Wrong: exporting is displacement.

standard.question: What does 'industrial symbiosis' mean within a circular economy?
options: One firm's waste or by-product becomes another firm's raw material
Firms competing to extract the most virgin resources
Burning all industrial waste for energy
Exporting industrial waste to other countries
optionExplanations: Correct: it closes loops between firms, turning outputs into inputs.
Wrong: that is linear competition for virgin material.
Wrong: combustion is recovery, not symbiosis.
Wrong: exporting waste is displacement, not symbiosis.

hard.question: Industrial symbiosis advances circularity because it...
options: turns one firm's by-products into another's feedstock, closing technical loops
intensifies competition for virgin inputs
relies on incinerating industrial waste
depends on exporting waste abroad
optionExplanations: Correct: it cycles materials between firms rather than discarding them.
Wrong: that is linear competition.
Wrong: combustion is not symbiosis.
Wrong: export is displacement.

6.title: Return and Refund
6.brief: A deposit-return law would add a small refundable charge to every bottle and can — pay it at purchase, get it back when you return the empty. Where it's been tried, return rates have soared.
6.caseFact: Deposit-return drives high reuse/recycling rates where collection works.

actions.label: Pass a full deposit-return law
actions.resultExplain: Proven, popular, high return rates Needs collection infrastructure.
actions.plainMeaning: Add refundable deposits to drinks containers citywide.
actions.conceptLink: deposit-return schemes driving reuse and recycling.

actions.label: Pilot it in one district
actions.resultExplain: Low-risk trial. Limited reach.
actions.plainMeaning: Test the scheme small-scale first.
actions.conceptLink: deposit-return schemes driving reuse and recycling.

actions.label: Rely on voluntary recycling
actions.resultExplain: No new system. Return rates stay low.
actions.plainMeaning: Skip the deposit.
actions.conceptLink: deposit-return schemes driving reuse and recycling.

justify.question: Why do container-deposit schemes achieve high return rates?
options: A refundable deposit gives a direct financial incentive to return the container
Deposits are punitive fines for buying drinks
They ban the sale of containers outright
Consumers ignore deposits entirely
optionExplanations: Correct: people return items to reclaim their money, so capture rates rise.
Wrong: it is a refundable deposit, not a fine.
Wrong: sales continue; only a deposit is added.
Wrong: the monetary incentive ensures most people do respond.

6.scene: A deposit-return law would add a small refundable charge to every bottle and can — pay it at purchase, get it back when you return the empty. Where it's been tried, return rates have soared.
6.conceptLink: deposit-return schemes driving reuse and recycling.

easy.question: Why do bottle deposits boost returns?
options: People return them to get money back
Deposits are fines
Bottles are banned
People ignore them
optionExplanations: Right: a refund is a direct incentive.
Wrong: it's refundable, not a fine.
Wrong: sales continue.
Wrong: most people respond to the refund.

standard.question: Why do container-deposit schemes achieve high return rates?
options: A refundable deposit gives a direct financial incentive to return the container
Deposits are punitive fines for buying drinks
They ban the sale of containers outright
Consumers ignore deposits entirely
optionExplanations: Correct: people return items to reclaim their money, so capture rates rise.
Wrong: it is a refundable deposit, not a fine.
Wrong: sales continue; only a deposit is added.
Wrong: the monetary incentive ensures most people do respond.

hard.question: Deposit-return schemes achieve high recovery because they...
options: attach a refundable financial incentive to returning containers
impose punitive fines on purchases
prohibit container sales
assume altruism without any incentive
optionExplanations: Correct: the refund directly motivates return.

6.title: Close the Hole
6.brief: You can ban dumping anything that could be recycled or burned instead — forcing the city to choose better options. It only works because you've built the alternatives; now you can shut the easy escape hatch.
6.caseFact: Denmark made it illegal to landfill waste that can be incinerated.

actions.label: Ban landfilling of recyclables and combustibles
actions.resultExplain: Forces waste up the hierarchy Only works if you've built the alternatives.
actions.plainLabel: Ban landfilling of recyclables and combustibles
actions.plainMeaning: Forbid dumping anything that can be recovered.
cons: Only works if you've built the alternatives.
actions.conceptLink: banning landfilling of recyclable/combustible waste.

actions.label: Phase the ban in by material
actions.resultExplain: A smoother, gradual transition. Slower to take effect.
actions.plainLabel: Phase the ban in by material
actions.plainMeaning: Ban materials one at a time.
actions.conceptLink: banning landfilling of recyclable/combustible waste.

actions.label: No ban
actions.resultExplain: Keeps the easy option open. Circular progress stalls.
actions.plainMeaning: Keep landfilling allowed.
actions.conceptLink: banning landfilling of recyclable/combustible waste.

justify.question: Why is banning the landfilling of recyclable/combustible waste only effective once alternatives exist?
options: A ban forces waste up the hierarchy, but only works if recovery capacity can absorb the diverted material
Landfill is the ideal destination for such waste
Bans always increase the total waste generated
Bans are universally ineffective
optionExplanations: Correct: without recycling/recovery in place, a ban creates a disposal gap rather than progress.
Wrong: the ban exists precisely to move waste off landfill.
Wrong: a ban targets destination, not generation.
Wrong: paired with alternatives, bans are effective.

6.scene: You can ban dumping anything that could be recycled or burned instead — forcing the city to choose better options. It only works because you've built the alternatives; now you can shut the easy escape hatch.

easy.question: Why ban dumping recyclables only when alternatives exist?
options: Waste needs somewhere better to go first
Landfill is best for them
Bans raise waste
Bans never work
optionExplanations: Right: without recovery in place, a ban creates a gap.
Wrong: the ban moves waste off landfill.
Wrong: a ban targets destination.
Wrong: with alternatives, bans work.

standard.question: Why is banning the landfilling of recyclable/combustible waste only effective once alternatives exist?
options: A ban forces waste up the hierarchy, but only works if recovery capacity can absorb the diverted material
Landfill is the ideal destination for such waste

Bans are universally ineffective
optionExplanations: Correct: without recycling/recovery in place, a ban creates a disposal gap rather than progress.

Wrong: a ban targets destination, not generation.
Wrong: paired with alternatives, bans are effective.

hard.question: A landfill ban on recyclables is only effective if...
options: sufficient recovery and treatment capacity exists to absorb diverted waste
landfill remains the preferred destination
no alternatives are developed

6.title: The Circular Workforce
6.brief: A circular economy needs people who can repair, sort, recover and remanufacture — so you can invest in training a whole new green workforce. It's a chance to make sustainability a source of good jobs.
6.caseFact: Circular activities (repair, recovery, remanufacture) create local employment.

actions.label: Launch a city-wide green-skills programme
actions.resultExplain: Builds the workforce circularity needs Training costs.
actions.plainMeaning: Train residents for repair, recycling and remanufacturing jobs.
actions.conceptLink: the jobs and skills a circular economy creates.

actions.label: Subsidise circular start-ups
actions.resultExplain: Seeds innovation. Narrower than broad training.
actions.plainMeaning: Fund new businesses in the circular economy.
actions.conceptLink: the jobs and skills a circular economy creates.

actions.label: No programme
actions.resultExplain: No spend. The transition lacks skilled workers.
actions.plainMeaning: Don't invest in skills.
actions.conceptLink: the jobs and skills a circular economy creates.

justify.question: Why does a circular economy require investment in the workforce?
options: Repair, recovery and remanufacturing are labour-intensive activities that need new skills
Circular systems operate entirely without people
Employment is irrelevant to circularity
A circular economy necessarily reduces total employment
optionExplanations: Correct: circular activities create skilled jobs, so workforce development underpins the transition.
Wrong: these activities are people-intensive.
Wrong: labour is central to circular operations.
Wrong: circular sectors tend to add, not cut, jobs.

6.scene: A circular economy needs people who can repair, sort, recover and remanufacture — so you can invest in training a whole new green workforce. It's a chance to make sustainability a source of good jobs.

It needs no people

optionExplanations: Right: circular work is labour-intensive.

A circular economy necessarily reduces total employment
optionExplanations: Correct: circular activities create skilled jobs, so workforce development underpins the transition.
Wrong: these activities are people-intensive.
Wrong: labour is central to circular operations.
Wrong: circular sectors tend to add, not cut, jobs.

hard.question: Why is workforce development integral to a circular transition?
options: Repair, recovery and remanufacturing are labour- and skill-intensive activities
Circular systems are fully automated
Employment is irrelevant to circularity
Circularity reduces total employment
optionExplanations: Correct: circular sectors need skilled people, so training underpins the shift.
Wrong: they are people-intensive.
Wrong: labour is central.
Wrong: circularity tends to add jobs.

6.title: Make Makers Pay
6.brief: Why should the city foot the bill for disposing of products companies sell? An "extended producer responsibility" law would make manufacturers pay to handle their goods at end of life — and design them to be recyclable in the first place.
6.caseFact: Polluter-Pays logic extends to producers funding the disposal of what they sell.

actions.label: Bring in full producer responsibility
actions.resultExplain: Shifts cost and design incentives onto producers Industry friction.
actions.plainMeaning: Make manufacturers fund the end-of-life of what they sell.
pros: Shifts cost and design incentives onto producers
actions.conceptLink: extended producer responsibility (EPR).

actions.label: Apply it to packaging and electronics only
actions.resultExplain: Tackles the biggest problems. Narrower scope.
actions.plainLabel: Apply it to packaging and electronics only
actions.plainMeaning: Target the worst waste streams first.
actions.conceptLink: extended producer responsibility (EPR).

actions.label: Decline
actions.resultExplain: Avoids industry fights. The city keeps paying the bill.
actions.plainMeaning: Don't introduce EPR.
cons: The city keeps paying the bill.
actions.conceptLink: extended producer responsibility (EPR).

justify.question: What behavioural change does Extended Producer Responsibility (EPR) aim to drive?
options: Making producers fund end-of-life handling incentivises durable, recyclable product design
Shifting all disposal costs onto consumers only
Banning the production of goods entirely
It has no effect on how products are designed
optionExplanations: Correct: when makers pay for disposal, they have a reason to design for recyclability and longevity.
Wrong: EPR places responsibility on producers, not solely consumers.
Wrong: it reshapes design and cost, not production itself.
Wrong: internalising end-of-life cost is specifically intended to change design.

6.scene: Why should the city foot the bill for disposing of products companies sell? An "extended producer responsibility" law would make manufacturers pay to handle their goods at end of life — and design them to be recyclable in the first place.

easy.question: What does making producers pay for disposal encourage?

Consumers paying only

Wrong: it shifts cost to producers.

6.title: Putting It to the People

actions.label: Campaign honestly for the levy
actions.resultExplain: Wins lasting public consent Risky if trust is already low.
actions.plainMeaning: Make the open, transparent case to voters.
cons: Risky if trust is already low.

actions.label: Water it down to ensure it passes

actions.plainLabel: Water it down to ensure it passes

actions.label: Skip the vote and impose it

justify.question: Why does the level of public trust shape which waste policies are politically feasible?
options: Citizens must consent to and cooperate with schemes for them to function
Effective policy can simply ignore public opinion
Higher trust reduces recycling participation
Public votes have no bearing on implementation
optionExplanations: Correct: waste policy relies on compliance and participation, which depend on trust.
Wrong: ignoring opinion invites resistance and non-compliance.
Wrong: trust supports participation.
Wrong: democratic consent strongly affects what can be enacted.

6.title: Pay As You Throw
6.brief: New technology lets you charge households by exactly how much rubbish they throw out, with smart bins tracking it. People who waste less pay less — a direct, personal incentive to cut down. You just have to keep it fair.

actions.label: Roll out smart bins and pay-as-you-throw
actions.resultExplain: Strong personal incentive to reduce and sort Must be designed carefully so it's fair to poorer households.
actions.plainLabel: Roll out smart bins and pay-as-you-throw

actions.label: Use smart bins for monitoring only

justify.question: Why does 'pay-as-you-throw' cut household waste, and what design risk must be managed?
options: Charging by volume incentivises reduction and sorting, but it must stay fair to poorer households
It is free, so it changes nothing
It works by banning the use of bins
It increases the amount households throw away
optionExplanations: Correct: volume-based charging rewards waste reduction, but equity must be designed in to avoid unfair burdens.
Wrong: it is a pricing mechanism, not free.
Wrong: it prices waste; it does not ban bins.
Wrong: it reduces, not increases, household waste.

6.title: The Final Audit

actions.label: Publish the results and commit to keep improving

pays off most for circular cities.

justify.question: What ultimately distinguishes a circular 'model' city from a linear one?

Wrong: population does not determine metabolism type.

Wrong: more landfills indicate a linear, not circular, system.
